Academic Affairs section is the institutional office that oversees a variety of academic programs and departments.

The section plays a pivotal role in advising students and assisting them as they navigate their academic pathway towards completing degree. The Academic Affairs facilitates students with a whole range of academic functions including admission, pre- registration, registration, examination & grading function and processing student requests.

The section is responsible for the implementation of the decisions taken on academic matters by the Senate.

IIT Jammu started its journey in the year 2016 with 3 UG programs and has grown now offering UG in 7 programmes, M.Tech in 8 specializations, M.Sc./MS(R) in 2 programmes and Ph.D. in 11 programmes. MSc & MS(R) Chemistry
About the Programme
The department of chemistry would like to launch its PG program, i.e. MSc & MS(R) from
academic year, 2022-23. The admissions to this PG program will be done through the Joint
Admission (JAM) entrance examination.
The main objective of the MSc & MS(R) Chemistry program is to absorb the best talent
(20 students in total) after their bachelor degree across the country. The IIT Jammu PG course in
Chemistry will provide them the necessary fundamental knowledge in both, theory and laboratory
practical levels. In addition to a strong foundation in all disciplines of chemistry, adequate exposure
to all analytical instruments in the state-of-the art instrument facility set up by IIT Jammu will be
provided. Given the expertise of the faculty in the Department presently, the students will have a
choice of cutting edge research in chemistry and related inter-disciplinary areas of research. The
program is envisioned to have core chemistry c

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To provide requisite foundations in quantum mechanics in the fields in chemistry, in particularly at
understanding bonding in molecules and solids
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
- The students should be able to account for the basic principles and concepts of quantum mechanics
- Describe the structure of the periodic system and the connections between the properties of the
elements and their electron configurations
- Describe the chemical bonding quantum mechanically with molecular orbital theory

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach physical organic chemistry and stereochemistry. The students
must understand and digest the fundamentals of physical organic chemistry to excel in their research
career. These include structure and bonding of organic molecules, the interrelationship between structure
and organic reactivity, the nature of reactive intermediates, and the study of organic reaction
mechanisms. A thorough command over these is necessary to read and understand the modern research
literature and problems. The course will be a toolbox for the students to solve their research problems
that they may confront during their research career.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
1. Comprehend and predict the role of temperature, solvents, and catalysts in organic reactions.
2. Elucidate reaction mechanisms using isotope effects.
3. Identify and differentiate prochirality and chirality

text: <!-- Page 17 --> 11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words): This course provides the complete knowledge of metal-coordination complexes and provides the understanding of the basic terminologies, theories explaining the formation and properties, electronic spectra, reactivity and stability, reaction mechanism and magnetic properties of metal-coordination complexes. 12. Course outcomes (about 100 words): At the end of this course, the students will be able to - name coordination compounds and to be able to draw the structure based on their name. - recognize the types of isomers in coordination compounds - understand the key features of coordination compounds, - use CFT to understand the magnetic properties of coordination compounds. - predict the shapes and structures of coordination complexes with coordination numbers. - describe the stability of metal complexes by the use of formation constants and to calculate thermodynamic parameters from them. 13. 11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach NMR spectroscopy, EPR spectroscopy, Electronic Spectrometry,
etc.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
This course enables to the students learn the basic of spectroscopic methods like 1 H-NMR, 13C-NMR,
Electronic and Vibrational spectroscopy, and their applications. This course gives idea of structure
determination of known and unknown organic molecules by using spectroscopic data.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities):
Electromagnetic radiation and its interaction with matter, natural line width, spectral intensity -
transition probability, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution.
Electronic spectroscopy - Principles, Beer-Lambert law, transitions among ligand orbitals, charge
transfer transition, Jablonski diagram, Frank-Condon principle, photoelectron spectroscopy,

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (About 100 words):
This course is designed to provide the knowledge and hands-on experience of the basic
laboratory techniques used for the synthesis, purification, and characterization of inorganic
complexes. In addition to it, this course provides an understanding of various analytical methods
for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of given chemical samples.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course, the students will be able to
- synthesize various coordination complexes.
- characterize the properties of coordination complexes through different methods.
- have the knowledge of qualitative and quantitative estimation of synthesized compounds.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Transition metal complexes have widespread application ranging from Biology to Materials
development to Organometallic catalysis in Industry as well as in Academia. Knowledge on the
organometallic compounds, their structure, bonding and applications in organometallic catalysis
will certainly help students to augment their knowledge in these areas of
chemistry.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After attending this course, Masters student will learn in details how complexes are use in the
synthesis of new molecules and organic transformation relevant to academia and industry. In this
course student will learn the role of electronic structure and geommetic structure in determining
the reactivity which is essential for further higher studies.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach about fundamental organic reactions and their applications in
advanced organic synthesis with emphasis on contemporary organic synthesis.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
1.The student will acquire advance knowledge of making C-C single bonds, double bonds, triple bonds;
and carbon-heteroatom bonds.
2.The student will understand how to design a molecule by using simple carbon-carbon bond forming
reactions and rearrangement reactions.
3.Student will learn the methods of constructing a variety of heterocycles.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities):
Reactions forming new C-C single bonds, double bonds, triple bonds, and carbon-heteroatom
bonds; Asymmetric addition of Grignard reagents, organozinc and lithium reagents to carbonyl
compounds; Directed lithiation

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To get exposed on the relevance of Chemistry with Biology. It will also make the student aware
how transition metal complexes are relevant in Biology.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
The students will get exposed with the advanced literature and knowledge on biocatalytic
reactions, Transition metal chemistry in biology. Student will also get knowledge on the design
and synthesis principle to the molecules.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To impart knowledge on the underlying dynamics and energetics of many chemical processes. To
introduce students with basic mechanism of the transformation of energy into different forms and its
relation to physical observable such as temperature, pressure, and volume. To provide fundamental
concepts about the rates and mechanisms of different chemical reactions.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
- The students should be able to use the basic concepts of thermodynamics and kinetics to real
life problems.
- Use the concept of chemical equilibrium, potential energy surface, and reaction dynamics to
solve complex chemical problems.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach and train students regarding various laboratory techniques related
to organic chemistry. This course has the following objectives: -
 Understanding various chromatography techniques including thin layer chromatography and
column chromatography.
 Understanding the execution of single step and multi-step organic reactions for the synthesis of
organic moieties.
 Understanding the basic techniques like distillation and crystallization.
 Understanding the reactivity, substitution and coupling of various organic substrates.
 To be able to determine the structure of isolated pure compounds using the spectroscopic
techniques.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Students would learn about synthesis of various organic compounds using single and multi-step reactions.
Students would get skillful in separating and purifying various organic compounds. They woul

The primary objective of this course is to acquire basic concepts, principles, and techniques of
modern analytical chemistry that would empower students with an analytical mind set and the
abilities to solve diverse analytical problems in an efficient and quantitative way that conveys the
importance of accuracy and precision of the analytical results. On successful completion of this
course, students will be able:

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach about applications of UV, IR and NMR spectroscopy
including NMR properties, one-dimensional spectroscopy (1H, 13C, DEPT, NOE) and an
introduction to two-dimensional NMR (COSY, NOESY, and HSQC) and their use in structure
elucidation. Applications of mass spectral methods in structure determination of organic
compounds.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
1. Students would be better at solving various spectroscopy problems including UV (detection of
conjugated molecules), IR (frequencies in analyzing the functional group and strengths of
various types of bonds) and NMR (data in elucidating the structure) which can further help them
to solve and elucidate the spectroscopic data of various molecules which can further help in
structural determination of unknown compounds.
2. Students could elucidate the structures of the organic compounds using mass spectro

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course mainly designed for computation applications form pharma industry to applications
of catalysis aimed at developing new sustainable processes in chemical synthesis, and the
computational chemistry particularly applied to the understanding and improvement of these
processes.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
- The students should be able to use the concepts of computational chemistry (quantum
mechanics, QM/MM, molecular mechanics, Machine learning algorithms) taught in the class
to predict the reactivity of complex chemical systems.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach the fundamentals of asymmetric synthesis. It covers all the
available methods to synthesize enantioenriched molecules and their applications in total synthesis.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
1. Student will understand the types of chirality and stereochemical description.
2. Student would get knowledge about measurement of enantiomeric excess and assigning their absolute
stereochemistry.
3. Learn the importance of chiral auxiliary in asymmetric inductions and different methods in resolution
of racemic compounds.
4. An insight in to detailed development of asymmetric catalysis would be gained.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities):
Importance of chirality, optical activity, measurement of enantiomeric excess, determination of absolute
configuration, chir

12. Course Outcome:
 On completion of the course, the students would be aware of the overall
process of drug discovery.
 They would be able to understand the modes of action of drugs, know the
methods for design of new drugs, methods for optimization of drug activity
and properties and understand the pharmacokinetics of drugs and importance
of drug safety.
 On successful completion, the students will be able to contribute in the
discovery of new drugs and would be able to come up with synthetic methods
for new drugs using the knowledge of synthetic organic chemistry.
 Overall, the students will be aware of the basic biology and pharmacology of
drugs, synthesis of drugs, importance of drug safety, novelty, intellectual
property protection and the patent process.
13. Course Contents:
Unit I: Overview of drug discovery, Basics of Drug Action- Enzymes, proteins,
receptors, antagonist, agonist, Drug-receptor interactions, Drug action through
enzyme inhibition and recept

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The main objective of this course is to provide the knowledge of nano porous materials, their
properties, different classes, various techniques for their characterization, overview of some
interesting and emerging porous materials, different application of these materials and the future
importance of nano porous materials.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course, students will have the knowledge of nano porous materials like carbon,
zeolites, silica, covalent organic frameworks (COFs), Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and its
hybrids and they will be able to classify and characterize these nano porous materials. Further,
the students will also be familia

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Transition metal complexes have widespread application in Biology. Development of catalytic
process inspired by the biologically relevant enzymes and metalloproteins are highly desirable
for green chemical transformation. This course will certainly provide knowledge on the
development of transition metal complexes, their structure, bonding and
applications in Bio-mimetic for sustainable chemical transformations.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After attending this course, Masters student will learn in details how chemistry is related to
biology and everyday life. It will also provide an understanding of the role of transition metal
complexes in catalytic chemical transformation

This course has been intended to introduce students to various types of natural products present in
nature, their classifications, structure, synthesis, and biogenesis; Stereochemistry, reaction, and
conformation of different carbohydrates; chemistry of vitamins; synthesis of organic molecule by total
synthesis; practical activities of natural product extraction.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The students will masteries the Woodward-Hoffmann rules and their applications in concerted
pericyclic reactions. The concepts in the photochemical reactions will be presented. The course will
illustrate the importance of thermal and photochemical reactions in advanced organic synthesis
especially in constructing the complex molecules. The emphasis will be given on reaction mechanisms
and synthetic applications.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
1. Concepts in photochemistry will be presented that will lay the foundation to design the sustainable
photocatalytic reactions.
2. The significance of Woodward-Hoffmann rules in predicting the reactivity and stereoselectivity of
concerted reactions will be given.
3. The importance of frontier molecular orbitals in organic chemical reactions will be understood.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study book

text:

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course has been intended to teach supramolecular chemistry. Further, this course deals with the
concepts and applications of chemistry that is based on molecular recognition and inspired by
biological supramolecular structures and materials chemistry
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words)
By the end of the course, the student must be able to understand importance and recent advances
outlook of future prospective of supramolecular chemistry in the field of organic, metal-organic and
materials chemistry
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities):
Definition of supramolecular chemistry. Nature of binding interactions in supramolecular structures: ion-
ion, ion-dipole, dipole-dipole, H-bonding, cation-p, anion-p, p-p, and van der Waals interactions.
Synthesis and structure of crown ethers, lariat ethers,

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Msc Msc Msr Chemistry Curriculum Senators Suggetion R 210044 (2)

text: | 13. | Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical / Practice activities): Definition of supramolecular chemistry. Nature of binding interactions in supramolecular structures: ion- ion, ion-dipole, dipole-dipole, H-bonding, cation-p, anion-p, p-p, and van der Waals interactions. Synthesis and structure of crown ethers, lariat ethers, podands, cryptands, spherands, calixarenes, cyclodextrins, cyclophanes, cryptophanes, carcerands and hemicarcerands. Host-Guest interactions, pre- organization and complimentarity, lock and key analogy. Binding of cationic, anionic, ion pair and neutral guest molecules. Self-assembly molecules: design, synthesis, properties and applications of the molecules, self-assembling by H-bonding, metal-ligand interactions and other weak interactions, metallomacrocycles, catenanes, rotaxanes, helicates and knots. Applications of supramolecular chemistry towards molecular biology and materials chemistry.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The objective of this course is to introduce the students to the different aspects of chemistry
applied to food. It will help the students appreciate and understand the different chemical
reactions that govern the preservation of food. It will also provide an introduction to the
different aspects of food engineering. It will conclude with an approach towards
commercialization of food processing.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of the course the students will be able to
- Identify different biomolecules and their transformation.
- Nutritional value of food chemicals
- Degradation of food and its longitivity
Appreciate the design of industrial scale processing of food.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To get exposed on the relevance of Chemistry in the synthesis of polymers of various kinds. As
polymers are associated in everyday life how complexes are useful in their synthesis will be
discussed.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After attending this course, Masters student will learn in details different kind of process for the
synthesis of polymers and how these kinds of compounds are formed. Students will also learn
how these polymers are essential in everyday life. It will also provide an understanding of the
role of transition metal complexes in its synthesis.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practiceactivities):
CYL 406: Polymer Chemistry (3-0-0-3)
1. Introduction: Classification of polymers, intermolecular forces in polymers.
2. Synthesis of polymers: Synthesis of polymers involving p-block complexes

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course is intended to provide the fundamental scientific principles of nano chemistry by
providing the detailed overview of various synthetic strategies, characterization techniques and
applications of nanomaterials. Further, this course gives the information about the most
common and interesting nanomaterials and also describes the recent progress in the applications
of nanomaterial.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course the student will be able to
- understand the importance of nanoscience and technology and its importance in our day-to-
day life.
- characterize these materials by various techniques like XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TGA, etc
- have the kn

text: <!-- Page 2 --> VISION and MISSION Department of Electrical Engineering is interested to propose an M. Tech. programme in VLSI and Cyber-Physical Systems. Beginning in the first decade of 21st century and continuing today, computational and communication resources, tightly integrated with physical systems, shaping the new capabilities for the modern systems. The use of computational resources to achieve different capabilities is in existence for several decades. However, many modern systems have high degree of complexities and also the scale of these systems is large and/or has distributed nature. There are several domains where CPS examples can be seen e.g. Healthcare, Robotics, Automotive, Power Grid, Avionics and Transportation. Such system necessitates a deeper understanding of system behaviour and also provides feedback to augment system design to achieve overall objectives from the system. The VLSI and Cyber-Physical Systems M. Tech. program will cover the fundamentals and engine

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M.Tech. Vlsi Course Of Study

text: (SMDP-C2SD)" is an Umbrella programme initiated under Digital India Program in December 2014 at 60 Academic and Research & Development Institutions spread across the country including IITs, NITs, IISc, IIITs, etc. for the duration 2014-2020. The project aims to not only develop more than 50,000 Specialized Manpower in the area of VLSI/System Design at B.Tech, M.Tech & Ph.D. levels but also to design & develop proof-of-concept Working Prototypes. The fourth phase of the SMDP programme is about to come in the year 2022, and IIT Jammu would like to be part of it. The M.Tech. in VLSI and Cyber-Physical System will be helpful in generating the human resources and expertise in VLSI and CPS domain and match with the future SMDP objectives. All the required software needed for VLSI and the embedded system is supported through this project. SMDP also provides human resource support to execute, run, and maintain the lab facilities. Two human resources, namely Lab engineer and Research associate,

1. Program Objective and Expected Graduate Attributes
 To educate and train the graduates with knowledge and skills necessary to formulate, design
and solve problems in Analog, Digital & Mixed Signal VLSI system design, VLSI Signal
Processing, Real Time Embedded System design and Hardware Software Co-Design.
 To provide technical skills in software and hardware tools related to the design and
implementation of integrated Circuits, System on Chip for real time applications.
 To provide adequate practical training to meet the requirement of the Electronics industry.
 Ability to understand modern approaches to Model CPS.
 Appreciation of cross disciplinary interaction of cyber and Physical systems.
 Ability to use knowledge from machine learning, control theory to achieve overall objectives
of a given CPSs.
 To train the students to take up leadership roles in their career or to pursue higher education
and research.

will take 4 and 6 semesters to complete all the course requirements and obtain the M.Tech
degree. The rules for the students enrolled in M. Tech programme under RA category are the
same as that under TA category.

11. Will the course require any faculty to be associated
with?
12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The purpose of this course is to provide students with the skills, knowledge and attitudes
required to determine approximate numerical solutions to mathematical problems which cannot
always be solved by conventional analytical techniques, and to demonstrate the importance of
selecting the right numerical technique for a particular application, and carefully analysing and
interpreting the results obtained.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of the course, the student should be able to:
Identify initial and boundary value problems and choose appropriate numerical schemes.
Apply appropriate algorithms to solve selected problems, both manually and by writing
computer programs.
Analyse the errors obtained in the numerical solution of problems.
Using appropriate numerical methods, determine approximate solutions to systems

12. Course objectives: (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The aim of the course is to introduce the unified approach to deal with transport phenomena in
energy and momentum transfer applications. Starting from the Reynold transport theorem how
one can select the integral and differential approaches to analyze the real life applications will
be demonstrated.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course, the students will be able to appreciate the different needs of Eulerian
(CV) and Lagrangian (CM) approaches. Depending of the problem in hand, one would be able to
choose a proper system or control volume and select the integ

11. Will the course require any faculty to be associated
with?
12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Formation, at a medium level, in heat transfer by conduction, convection and radiation and fluid
dynamics: phenomenological aspects, mathematical formulation (basic conservation laws and
constitutive laws) and analytical and numerical resolution techniques. Methodologies of
resolution of problems of technological interest which different forms of combined heat transfer
are presented.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Basic concepts about momentum, heat and mass transfer. Introduction to general conservation
equations for transport phenomena.
Ability to improve mathematical models and use analytical methods for solving these
mathematical models for transport phenomena.

(Include Practical / Practice activities):

12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Upon successful completion, the students will be
- Able to understand the concepts of tunnel design and will be able to design a tunnel
- Able to decide the type of supporting system and design the system
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities): Introduction; Tunnel requirements - operational, structural; Surveying and
alignment in tunnels; Choice of tunnel system, shape and size of cross section, rock pressure
and cavity stabilization; Properties and types of supports, design of support systems, tunnel
lining.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
- To learn various geological features of earth
- To know the types of rocks and their formation, classification
- To know the engineering properties of rocks
- To know the failure theories of rock, rock mass classification theories
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After successful completion of course the students will be able to
- Know how the rocks form
- Know the properties of rock and how to measure them
- Know the rock mass classification and failure theories

Practice activities): Geology and its importance, Mineralogy, Classification of rocks - igneous rocks,
sedimentary rocks, metamorphic rocks, weathering of rocks, geological work of rivers, structural
geology, engineering properties of rocks, Introduction to rock mechanics, rock matrix and various
theories, In-situ st

12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
By the end of this course, the students would be able to
1. use fracture mechanics in design and analysis of flawed components
2. apply fracture mechanics concepts in tunnel engineering
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities):
Fracture: an overview, review of mechanics, Linear elastic fracture mechanics, Grifith’s crack
theory, stress analysis of crack, energy and stress field approaches, plane strain and plane
stress, fracture toughness testing, crack opening displacement, elasto- plastic analysis, J-
integral, ductile-brittle transition, impact energy, microstructural aspects of fracture toughness,
environmental assisted cracking, fatigue crack propagation, analysis of engineering failures,
Fracture of rock and concrete, Applications and case studies.

provided in the tunnels
- Design the protection features in tunnels by knowing the various hazards and safety issues
in tunnels

systems; ventilation in tunnels; hazards in tunneling; safety aspects during construction of
tunnels; safety aspects during operation of tunnels;

- Critically evaluate project failure factors and minimize their vulnerability
- Compare between qualitative and quantitative risk assessment tools
- Apply risk analysis methods to real life tunnel projects

- Differentiate between various contract types and select the appropriate type needed for a
particular project.
- Scrutinize contractor’s bids and select a reliable, competent contractor.
- Analyze the provisions made in the Contract Act and Arbitration act; interpret their
repercussions on the contract performance.
- Apply the FIDIC document provisions in contracting practice.

Practical / Practice activities): Project planning, surface conditions, design and construction
management, cost estimates, schedules, pricing and payment provisions, contracts, dispute
resolution, Contracts - Indian Contract Act (1872), FIDIC, Contractor selection, Indian
Arbitration and Conciliation Act,.

| VISION Technological developments in the field of communication have revolutionised the way people see the world today, by crossing the limitations of time and space. Recent advancements in communication, signal processing, microwave, VLSI, and computing have collectively enabled people to connect to the external world any-time, any-where. This has led to an era of enormous amount of information exchange in the form of data, images and videos. With ever-increasing expectations from users in the form of quality of service, the communication industry is looking forward to experts who could think out of the box, and suggest novel solutions to practical problems. In view of the growing demand of expertise in the field of communications and signal processing, IIT Jammu envisions an MTech Program to provide deeper understanding of the theoretical and application oriented practical concepts. The program aims at helps in providing strong foundation with required analytical co

Course Content (Core)
Signal Theory
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
The course is meant for graduate students to make them familiar with the signal theory. It
provides a review of axioms of probability, single and multivariate distributions, and functions of
​
random variables, mean-squared convergence, convergence in distribution, the central limit theorem,
random waveforms, signal spaces,autocorrelation and the power spectrum, random processes.
Assessment Criterion
1)Laboratory : 30 %
2) Examination : 50 %
3) Course Work : 20 %
MODULE 1 10 Hours
Discrete random variables (Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson, geometric, negative binomial, etc.) and
their properties like PDF, CDF, MGF.
MODULE 2 10 Hours
Continuous random variables: Gaussian, multivariate Gaussian; whitening of the Gaussian
random vector; complex Gaussian random vector, circularity; Rayleigh and Rician; exponential;
chi-squared; gamma.
MODULE 3 10 Hours
Signal spaces: convergence and continuity; linear spaces, inner

Detection and Estimation theory
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
The course is broadly divided into three parts. The first part will deal with the design,
implementation and performance evaluation of detectors; this would cover composite and M-ary
hypothesis testing. The second part of the course deals with estimation techniques like Maximum
Likelihood, Linear minimum variance estimation, Least squares methods CRLB for random
​
parameter estimation. The third part introduces with the detection techniques; this will cover K-L
​ ​
expansion and its application to Detection of known and un-known (i.e. with unknown,
parameters) signals in AWGN, Wiener filter.
​
Assessment Criterion
1)Laboratory : 30 %
2) Examination : 50 %
3) Course Work : 20 %
MODULE 1 14 Hours
Overview

Digital Communication
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
The course is meant for graduate level students and covers in detail receiver designs for digital
communications using statistical communication theory principles and Signal Space concepts for
optimum receiver design. It will also involve analyzing advance digital communication system
analytically and using MATLAB simulation. The course also covers digital wireless multiple
access systems techniques like CDMA & OFDM. MIMO systems will also be discussed to
achieve receive & transmit diversity and multiplexing gains.
Assessment Criterion
1)Laboratory : 30 %
2) Examination : 50 %
3) Course Work : 20 %

Introduction to Digital Communication

Antenna and Wave Propagation

The course will provide students with an in depth introduction to fundamental parameters of
antennas, friis and radar equations. The course also covers antenna arrays, linear wire and loop
antennas, antenna synthesis and antenna measurements. Different types of antennas are discussed
in great detail for the students.

2) Examination : 50%

Fundamental Parameters of Antennas

Antenna Efficiency, Gain, Beam Efficiency, Bandwidth, Polarization, Input Impedance, Antenna
Radiation Efficiency, Antenna Vector Effective Length and Equivalent Areas, Maximum

Adaptive Signal Processing
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
This post graduate course is designed to cover techniques for adaptive signal processing,
detection and parameter estimation. It will briefly introduce with the structure of adaptive filters,
issues and their applications. Discrete time stochastic processes are discussed. The course will
deal with the search methods, wiener filter and the LMS algorithm; LMS algorithm is covered in
great detail. The course will provide students knowledge about the vector space work for optimal
filtering, the lattice filter and estimator.

3) Course Work : 20 %

Introduction to machine learning. Classification: nearest neighbour, decision trees, perceptron,
support vector machines, VC-dimension.

Regression: linear least squares regression, support vector regression. Additional learning
problems: multiclass classification, ordinal regression, ranking.
MODULE 3 8 Hours
Ensemble methods: boosting. Probabilistic models: classification, regression, mixture models
(unconditional and conditional), parameter estimation, EM algorithm.
MODULE 4 10 Hours
Beyond IID, directed graphical models: hidden Markov models, Bayesian networks. Beyond IID,
undirected graphical models: Markov random fields, conditional random fields.
MODULE 5 8 Hours
Learning and inference in Bayesian networks and MRFs: parameter estimation, exact inference
(variable elimination, belief propagation), approximate inference (loopy belief propagation,
sampling). Additional topics: semi-supervised learning, active learning, structured prediction.

This course aims at providing thorough information of the conventional and upcoming satellite

communications like orbital theory and link budget analysis. The course would be taught from
physical layer signal processing point of view and it would cover teaching of famous channel
- -
model of the land mobile satellite (LMS) links and also some recently discovered channel
models. The multiple antennas based satellite communication would also be explained. The
hybrid satellite terrestrial satellite communication technology would also be introduced.

a M. Tech degree in Chemical Engineering or Chemical Engineering with
specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering with AI (starting in the academic

In the second year of the program, the student will involve themselves in research projects

Specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering with AI
The main objective of the specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering with AI is to
prepare the students for:
• Carry out research in energy systems such as renewable energy (Solar, Hydrogen, and
Ammonia), electrochemical energy conversion systems (fuel cells, batteries,
supercapacitors, hybrid devices and miniaturized devices), heat transfer, energy
intensive materials, hydrogen energy, kinetics and molecular modelling, green chemical
process, energy economics.
• Getting employment in public/private industrial energy sectors, Government energy
agencies, consultancy energy services and educational institution dealing with energy.
• Pursue for higher studies /employment in national and international institutions working
on energy and environment.
• To prepare students with the knowledge and application of AI in the field of energy engineering.
• Successfully carry out their profession as an entrepreneur and te

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (About 100 words):
To course aims to
• Provide students with the concepts, and laws of classical thermodynamics to
applications that require quantitative knowledge of thermodynamic properties
for different applications of green processing.
• To develop understanding of thermodynamic processes occurring in sustainable
chemical manufacturing.
• To understand multi-component interactions, practical examples and industrial
applications.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course, Masters and PhD students are expected to
- Formulate and manipulate phase equilibrium problems for complex systems based
on classical and molecular thermodynamics.
- Analyze specific chemical engineering problems using fundamental concepts.
- To relate thermodynamics, processes with practical problems and provide
innovative solutions.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study
boo

and hybrid modelling
Model predictive control, Data flow and measurements in the chemical industry

12. Course Outcome:
Students, after completion of the course, would be able to:
The study of energy topics involves both theory and practice. These topics include energy
generation, storage, efficiency, consumption, economics, and feasibility. Environmental
aspects of energy are also of great significance. The course intends to cater to the expected
environmental impacts of sustainable energy sources and propose potential remedies for the
anticipated consequences.

11. Course Objective:
The course helps students to understand underlying patterns and characteristics of
vast amounts of data collected from various sources by performing advanced data
analytics to transform data into understandable and actionable information for the
purpose of making decisions. This course develops students’ knowledge to improve
the economy and efficiency of systems, processes, and enterprises through data
analytics.

13. Course Contents:
Mathematical Models and Data Analysis, Probability Concepts and Probability Distributions,
Data Collection and Preliminary Data Analysis, Making Statistical Inferences from Samples,
Estimation of Linear Model Parameters Using Least Squares, Optimization Methods,
Classification and Clustering Methods, Parameter Estimation Methods, Tools and techniques
needed to collect, clean, analyze and present data specific to the

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty (Yes/No) (If yes, provide details)
11. Course Objective:
The basic purpose of this course is to introduce students to the basic concepts of
environmental engineering and waste management. It will unable students to
appreciate and design systems competent for tackling issue of waste management.
12. Course Outcome:
Students after completion of the course would be able to:
− Appreciate the environmental concerns and be aware of the
environmental norms in India.
− Be able to do material balances of different environmental systems.
− Design water treatment units for portable and waste water.
− Design air treatment units to combat air pollution and climate change.
− Design solid waste treatment unit to combat solid waste management.
13. Course Contents:
The course covers the concept of ecological balance and the contribution of industrial
and human activities in the changes of the environmental quality. Ecological cycles.
Conc

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty No
11. Course Objective: This course is to introduce the students about the emerging area
of microfluidics and its applications. The course familiarizes the students with
fundamentals of microfluidics and apply the principles in designing, modelling,
fabrication and development of lab on a chip devices for different applications.
12. Course Outcome:
At the end of this course, the students are introduced to
● The microfluidic principles and physics of fluid flows in micro-channels.
● Designing, fabrication techniques and characterization of different
components of microfluidic devices, their assembly and packaging.
● The emerging trends and advances in microfluidics and lab on a
chip technology.
● Laboratory modules in microfluidic devices

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M.Tech In Chemical Engineering With Specialisation In Sustainable Energy Engineering With Ai

text:
<!-- Page 75 -->
11. Course Objective
-Introduce fundamental concepts of combustion
-Emphasize on the importance of chemical kinetics, thermochemistry, and physics in
combustion problems
-Apply the concepts of chemical reaction engineering to analyze problems in
combustion
-Analyze laminar and turbulent flames and characterize their properties
-Understand the problem of combustion generated pollutant emissions and introduce
modern methods for control and prevention
12. Course Outcome:
After completing the course, students should
− become proficient in estimating thermochemical properties and handling
chemical kinetic mechanisms
− be able to identify the types of flames and select appropriate analysis methods
− gain capability to apply analytical methods to solve combustion problems
− be able to design laboratory scale burners used to measure properties of flames
− be able to undertake and solve practical problems related to combustion
13 Course Content:
Introduction, Thermochemistry: s

text:
<!-- Page 76 -->
and destruction, droplet vaporization and burning, solid fuel combustion, Combustion
and pollution: sources of air pollution and its ill effects on human health and
environment, oxides of nitrogen-chemical mechanism and control strategies, carbon
monoxide, unburnt hydrocarbons, quantification of emissions, combustion modification
methods

text:

11. Course Objective:
This course presents a basic understanding of data science, artificial intelligence and
machine learning for chemical engineering applications. Data science is finding
applications in various chemical engineering applications with the generation of rich data
from different chemical engineering processing plants and lab experiments. This course
will help students to understand and develop data science solutions for chemical
engineering applications.
12. Course Outcome:
Students undergoing this course will be able to understand the basics of data science
and its applications for different chemical engineering applications.
13. Course Contents:
Introduction to data science, artificial intelligence and machine learning, introduction
to Python for data science, data science and machine learning fundamentals, linear
algebra and optimization for data science and machine learning, Statistical foundations
for data science and machine learning, regression a

11. Course Objective:
Process Intensification is considered as the future of the chemical process industry. It
is an important course for new chemical engineers to adapt into working of the
modern chemical process industry.
12. Course Outcome:
Students after completion of the course would be able to:
● Design intensified equipment for a given unit operation or unit process or a
combination of the two.
● Design intensified plants for processing.
13. Course Contents:
Introduction to Process Intensification, strategic approaches to intensification,
protocol for design of intensified plant using tool box model, working of different
intensification equipment i.e. Static Mixers, HiGee contactor, micro HEX, Micro
reactor, oscillatory baffled reactor, reactive distillation, divided wall column,
membrane separation, heat pipes, spinning

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty NO
11. Course Objective:
The course aims to
● Learn a few basic numerical techniques (Finite Difference) to solve simple
ODEs/PDEs/algebraic equations derived from a variety of heat and mass
transfer related conservation equations.
● Analytical methods yield exact solutions, often in infinite series; use
mathematical functions.
● Numerical methods yield approximate solutions; use numbers, computations;
results are dependent on the method accuracies and limited by computers
machine errors.
● To learn the basics of MATLAB/Python programing and to write simple MATLAB
code.
12. Course Outcome:
Ensure that you are aware of the wide range of easily accessible numerical methods
that will be useful in your thesis research, at practice school, and in your career.
• Make you confident in your ability to look up and apply additional methods when
you need them.
• Help you become familiar with MATLAB, other convenient numerical softw

text:

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty (Yes/No) (If Yes, provide details)
11. Course Objective:
Natural circulation based passive heat transport systems are extensively used in
thermal, solar and nuclear power plants. The thermosyphon reboilers used in process
industries, transformer cooling and cooling of electronics systems are also based on
natural circulation. A participant in this course will learn the basic principles of
operation, transient and stability behavior of natural circulation based passive
systems.
12. Course Outcome:
The course would provide a detailed understanding of the natural circulation systems
used in thermal, solar and nuclear power plants. At the end of the course, the
students would be capable of performing design of natural circulation systems for a
specified heat transport capability. Besides, they will be able to perform transient
and
stability analysis of natural circulation based passive safety systems.
13. Course Contents:
N

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M.Tech In Chemical Engineering With Specialisation In Sustainable Energy Engineering With Ai

text:
<!-- Page 101 -->
fields, advantages and their analysis methods.
14. Brief Description of Modular Course Engagement (Tentative)
Module Topic Covered No. of
No. Hours
1 Natural circulation systems - brief history, working principle, 2
advantages and challenges, review of applications in commercial
systems and classification of natural circulation systems.
2 Analysis of natural circulation systems - governing equations for 8
single-phase, two-phase and supercritical closed loop and open loop
systems, steady state analysis of single-phase, two-phase and
supercritical systems, generalized dimensionless flow equation,
parametric effects and natural circulation performance of proposed
coolants for advanced nuclear and solar thermal power plants.
3 Transient analysis of single-phase, two-phase and supercritical 6
systems, numerical models, solution procedure and analysis of various
transients and parametric influences
4 Instability: Introduction to instability, classification of instabilities

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty (Yes/No) (If Yes, provide details)
11. Course Objective:
Fuel is an important part of energy studies. This course is designed to introduce the
students to the different areas of fuel processing and technologies related to fuel
utilization. Processing of raw materials to finished products.
12. Course Outcome:
After completion of the course the students will be able to
− Comprehend and solve problems related to basic transport phenomena
− Design of processes for fuel upgradation
− Design of processes for waste to fuel conversion
13. Course Contents:
Transport Phenomena and Reaction Engineering: analogy between momentum,
heat and mass transport, phase and reaction equilibrium thermodynamics, reaction
kinetics for homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, fundamental design of
reactors. Coal: Coal as a source of energy, types of coal, proximate and ultimate
analysis, carbonization of coal, gasification and liquefaction of coal

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
● To provide a reference for engineers on heat and fluid flow measurement techniques
and instruments.
● To signify a balance between laboratory ideas and the realities of field experience.
● To provide practical advice on the design, operation, and performance of experimental
techniques.
● To give a detailed review of essentials such as accuracy, flow, selection, calibration
methods, and data analysis.
● Understand on-field problems generated during experiments as well as data processing
and introduce modern methods for its control and prevention.

electrochemistry of fuel cells and batteries.
- Modeling and characterization of the fuel cell and batteries.
- Hydrogen energy, its generation, storage and transportation aspects.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course is designed to teach the synthesis of nanomaterials, basic principles of various
techniques used to characterize the nanomaterials, and the application of nanomaterials in
the field of energy storage.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
● After completing the course, students are expected to Have the knowledge of
nanomaterials and their application in energy storage systems
● Get acquainted with the basic working principles of various characterization
techniques
● Plan and execute projects by application of various characterization
techniques
● Able to analyze their samples by various characterization techniques

Module Topic Covered No. of
No. Hours
1 Introduction to Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology
3
Introduction, history, 0 D, 1D, 2D materials, carbon nanotubes,
graphene, nanowires, core-shell, metal, and metal oxide nanowires.
2 Synthesis of Nanomaterials 3
Top down and bottom-up approach
3 Characterization Techniques 26
Introduction of various characterization techniques (thermal analysis,
liquid Chromatography, Gas chromatography, Molecular Spectroscopy
i.e. UV/Visible, FTIR, Mass spectroscopy, XPS techniques, AFM
techniques, SEM, XRD, TEM, Auger electron spectroscopy, Laser light
scattering, etc.)
4 Introduction of Electrochemistry
5
Interdisciplinary nature of electrochemistry, cell potential, cell
thermodynamics, 3-electrode and 2- electrode cell formation,
overpotential, Nernst equation. Electrochemical characterization
techniques.

11. Course Objective: Green chemistry has been a forefront area of sustainable
development in the world. It is important that students be aware of the principles of
green chemistry and its industrial application. The course is envisioned to introduce
students to the area of green chemistry, processing using green engineering and
intensification of such processes.
12. Course Outcome:
Students after completion of the course would be able to:
− Justify chemical processing using green routes
− Design and develop biorefining processes
13. Course Contents:
Tools of Green Chemistry, Principles of Green Chemistry, Green Chemistry Metrics:
Material Efficiency and Strategic Synthesis Design, Evaluating Feedstocks and Starting
Materials and Biorefining, Evaluating Reaction and Reactor Types, Evaluation of
Methods to Design Safer Chemicals, Future Trends in Green Chemical Processing

11. Course Objective:
The present course in true sense is an interdisciplinary subject where biologist,
physicist, chemist, engineers and mathematician all join hand in hand to develop
bioprocesses. This specialized subject comprises of transport processes, reaction
engineering and advanced control system besides conventional biology, physics and
mathematics.
12. Course Outcome:
Students undergoing this course will be able to develop the expertise to do the
design of any biochemical process.
13. Course Contents:
Biochemistry and Bioproducts, Stoichiometry and Thermodynamics of biochemical
reactions, Kinetics of homogeneous chemical reactions, Different types of bioreactors
and reactor analysis, batch, fed-batch, repeated fed-batch, CSTR, CSTR with recycle,
multistage CSTRs, and PFR, rheology of fermentation fluids; mixing and scale-up; air
sterilization; media sterilization; design of f

10. Will the course require any visiting faculty No
11. Course Objective:
The present course is designed to teach fundamentals of chemical process safety and
hazards management, the advancement in the field of risk assessment (both
intentional and unintentional threats) and to provide future perspective of inherently
safer processes and designs for making safe process plants. It covers the most
important concepts: static electricity, intensity of thermal radiation, thermodynamics
of fluid phase equilibria, boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion (BLEVE), emission
source models, hazard identification methods, risk control and methods for achieving
manufacturing excellence while also focusing on safety.
12. Course Outcome:
Students undergoing this course will he students will be equipped with concepts of
engineering systems safety, dimensions of engineering systems safety, design for
engineering systems safety and control for safety. They will also understand the
essen

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This is an introductory course for students who would like to work in the domain of
biochemical, biological or biomedical engineering. The course is designed to
introduce the concepts of momentum transport and mass transport to students.
The examples that would be covered will comprise of botanical and zoological
systems.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After completion of the course the students will be able to
● Appreciate the analogy between momentum, energy and mass
transport.
● Develop flow equation for Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids.
● Develop equation for single and multi-component transport in biological
systems

booklet) (Include Practical / Practice activities):

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course is designed to introduce students to the interdisciplinary approach
used in design and development of Biosensors. The course would start with
introduction to the basic design principles, and the fundamental principles
followed by the applications to design chemical, biological and engineering
concepts. Equal emphasis as well as the applications of the subject matter. The
student would be introduced to the bio-sensing application into the design of
most commonly used diagnostic assays which can be categorized as
immunoassays and nucleic acid amplification assay.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After successful completion of the course, students are expected to understand
the fundamental design principles of various kind of biosensors

14. Detailed Contents
Module Topic No. of
No. Hours
1 Introduction to Biosensors: A brief history, definitions, biological 10
inspiration, types of sensors, target analytes, various recognition events,
Bio Affinity: Labeled and Label free whole cell sensing bacteria, yeast,
mammalian cell, Biomolecule immobilization techniques.
2 Basic design and transduction mechanisms: Types of Transduction 12
mechanism, Optical; Electrochemical and Mass based which includes
Colorimetric, ECL, Surface Plasmon Resonance, Electro chemical; FET,
Impedance, Piezoelectric; Cantilever, fluorescence calibration, dynamic
Range, signal to noise, sensitivity, selectivity, Interference
3 Applications: Micro fabricated biosensors and point-of-care diagnostics 12
systems, Noninvasive biosensors in clinical analysis, Biosensors for early
diagnostics, Disease Managements such its use in Diabetes
management, cancer management and other diseases.
4 Special topics and future scope: State-of-art techn

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (About 100 words):
Reactor and Process Design in Sustainable Energy Engineering is a course designed as a Department
Core subject in the Chemical Engineering discipline to be offered to the Postgraduate students. The
significance of the subject is evident from the fact that at the undergraduate level students learn
transport phenomena, the working and design of the ideal reactors. This subject will briefly give an
overview of the basics of Chemical Reaction Engineering and Transport Phenomena. Reactor design
parameters that are related to transport phenomena and reaction kinetics would be taught. There
will be case studies covered to design microfluidic reactors and industrial reactors for sustainable
energy production.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After successful completion of the course, students would be able to understand the complex nature
of chemical reactors and methodology followed to design

for Master of Technology (M.Tech.)
in
Civil Engineering with specialization in
Geotechnical Engineering
Department of Civil Engineering
Indian Institute of Technology Jammu

---

<!-- Page 2 -->
Vision
Nestled in the Himalayas, the IIT Jammu fraternity is well aware of the unique challenges
posed by the region's complex geology, seismic activity, and rugged terrain. These challenges
significantly impact infrastructure development in the area. Therefore,

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M.Tech In Civil Engineering With Specialization In Geotechnical Engineering

text:
<!-- Page 3 -->
Master of Technology in Civil Engineering with Specialization in Geotechnical
Engineering
Guidelines
While drafting the course structure and curriculum following guidelines provided by the
academic section of IIT Jammu is followed.
1) Total credits of the M.Tech. program are in the range of 60-65.
2) The credits allocated for the program core (PC) courses should be in the range of 20
30 % of the total credits of the M.Tech. program.
3) The credits allocated for the program elective (PE) courses should be in the range of 20
30 % of the total credits of the M.Tech. program. At least one PE course should be
present in 3rd semester for 2-year programs and in 5th semester for a 3-year program.
4) The credits allocated for the dissertation should be in the range of 40 45 % of the total
credits of the M.Tech. program.
5) There should be at least 3 credits for the practical course.
6) There should be at least 2 credits for co-curricular activities.

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. Vlsi Design Updated Curriculum

text: <!-- Page 2 --> VISION and MISSION The Department of Electrical Engineering is interested in proposing an M. Tech. programme in VLSI Design. The VLSI Design M. Tech. program will cover the fundamentals and engineering aspect of microelectronics device designing, VLSI technology, and developing integrating circuits. The program will focus on developing hands-on skill of designing semiconductor devices and circuits, tapeout and testing, and architecting systems using embedded components. The program will enable engineers with required competency to design software-hardware architecture. 1. Relevance of the program The Ministry of Electronics & Information technology (MeitY), Government of India has launched the first phase of Special Manpower Development Programme (SMDP-I) for the duration 1998-2005 where 7 Resource Centres and 12 Participating Institutes were the implementing organizations. The second phase of the Special Manpower Development Programme (SMDP-II) was for the duration 200

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. Vlsi Design Updated Curriculum

text: and train the graduates with knowledge and skills necessary to formulate, design and solve problems in Analog, Digital & Mixed Signal VLSI system design, VLSI Signal Processing, Real Time Embedded System design and Hardware Software Co-Design. ● To provide technical skills in software and hardware tools related to the design and implementation of integrated Circuits, System on Chip for real time applications. ● To provide adequate practical training to meet the requirements of the Electronics industry. ● To train the students to take up leadership roles in their career or to pursue higher education and research. ● To provide scope for Applied Research and innovation in the various fields of VLSI, and enabling the students to work in the emerging areas. ● To enhance communication and soft skills of students to make them work effectively as a team.

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. Vlsi Design Updated Curriculum

text:
---

<!-- Page 3 -->
3. Program Learning Outcomes
On completion of the program, the graduates will:
● Be able to analyse, design and implement Analog, Digital and Mixed Signal Circuits and real
time Cyber-Physical Systems.
● Have in-depth knowledge and capability to use industry standard tools in the design and
implementation of VLSI and real time Cyber-Physical Systems.
● Capability to co-design hardware-software architecture in distributed environments.
● Methods to embedded security in systems.
● Be able to undertake research projects in related domains of VLSI Design.
● Possess the capability to communicate effectively and work as a team in the professional
career.
4. Opportunities in Industry
Following are some companies related to VLSI and Cyber-Physical Systems:

Qualcomm Macom NXP Semiconductor

text: <!-- Page 2 --> To cater the M.Tech CSE (with Specialization in Data Sciences (DS) or Information Security (IS)) at IIT Jammu for Defence Personnel without having background degree in Computer Science / Electronics, the following curriculum has been developed. This curriculum will provide the essential background knowledge for the students to successfully complete the degree program. The remedial programme includes some core subjects of Computer Science and they are highlighted in the semester wise course schedule. Students have to complete these 4 remedial courses in addition to the existing set of courses for M.Tech CSE in DS / IS as run in IIT Jammu. Students also need to do a project work that would start from Semester 2 and continue until the Summer Break of Semester 3. This project work will supplement the requirement of normal dissertation work for M.Tech students. The M.Tech (CSE) programme in IIT Jammu is taught jointly by IIT Jammu and DAIICT Gandhinagar. text: <!-- Page 5 --> programming constructs, syntax and semantics. In this course they will be using them to understand the way of organizing and structuring the data in linear and nonlinear ways through static and dynamic data structures. Students will understand the importance of different data structures such as arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs and their applications in the solving of real time computational problems. Students will also understand the reason we need good algorithms with practical exposure to running time of algorithms and their complexity evaluation. 12. Course Outcome: At the end of the course the student will be able to understand linear and non- linear data structure and will be able to efficiently store, organize, manipulate and delete data using efficient data structures. Students will be able to understand the importance of writing good algorithms in terms of time and space complexity. Students will get the knowledge of object oriented programmin

Data Organization and Retrieval
What is information retrieval, Function overview, Relationships between Digital library and IRS,
Introduction to Information Retrieval by Manning, Data control and data presentation, Boolean
system/model, Information Storage and Retrieval Systems by Korflage, Vector retrieval
system/model, Information Retrieval by Manning, Probability Retrieval System/model, Automatic
indexing and abstracting, Similarity measure algorithms, Automatic clustering approaches,
Information Visualization, Internet Information Retrieval, Image Retrieval

35(3):243-255.

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 16 -->
Advanced Digital Signal Processing
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
The course will briefly provide the review of signals and systems. It explains about digital filtering
which involves design and quantization effects of digital filters. Multirate filterings, Cyclostationarity
and LPTV filters and wavelet transform are covered in the course.
Assessment Criterion
1)Laboratory : 30 %
2) Examination : 50 %
3) Course Work : 20 %
MODULE 1
Review of Signals and Systems: This module includes Band Pass Sampling and data
reconstruction processes. Z Transform, Definition of Z Transform, Importance of Z transform
over Fourier Transform, Properties of Z Transform and Inverse Z Transform. Discrete linear
systems.
MODULE 2
Frequency domain design of Digital filters: This module includes design of FIR and IIR filters
in frequency domain.
MODULE 3
Quantization effects in Digital Filters : This module includes Distribution of Truncation
Errors, Quantization of Filter Coefficients and Quantizat

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 17 -->
Computer Networks
3-0-2
Learning Outcomes:
At the end of this course, students will be able to
1. Understand basic data communication system and network technology
2. Identify the different types of network topologies and protocols, exposure of
the layers of the OSI model
3. Apply mathematical foundations to solve computational problems in
communication networks
4. Identify and understand various routing and access control techniques in
network layer with routing algorithms
5. Identify and understand various switching techniques and digital switching
network.
Assesment Criterion:
Theory: 70%
Laboratory: 30%
Course Content:
Module I: Introduction and overview: Types of Networks; Circuit and Packet
switching, Interconnection of networks, Layered communication Architecture, Network
model ISO-OSI model, primitives and services, X 25 Protocol, System Network
Architecture, Stop and Wait Protocol, Go-Back-N Protocol, Throughput analysis and
Optimum packet length, High level d

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 19 -->
Introduction to IoT
1-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
At the end of this course, students will be able to
1. Understand the types of technologies that are available and in use today and
can be utilized to implement IoT solutions.
2. Apply these technologies to tackle scenarios in teams of using an experimental
platform for implementing prototypes and testing them as running applications.
Course Content:
Module I: Smart cities and IoT revolution, From IT to IoT, M2M and peer networking
concepts. Software Defined Networks (SDNs), Principles of Edge/P2P networking,
Protocols to support IoT communications, modular design and abstraction.
Module II: IOT networks (PAN, LAN and WAN), Edge resource, pooling and caching,
client-side control and configuration, Smart objects as building blocks for IoT.
Applications of IoT, connected cars IoT
Text Books:
[T1] A Bahaga, V. Madisetti, “Internet of Things- Hands on approach”, VPT
publisher, 2014.
[T2] A. McEwen, H. Cassimally, “Designing the

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 20 -->
Microwave and Millimeter Wave Integrated Circuits
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
Equip students with an understanding of microwave and millimeter wave integrated
circuits with a special emphasis on active circuits.
Assessment Criterion
1) TermPaper/mini Project : 30 %
2) Examination : 70 %
MODULE 1
Introduction to Microwaves and Millimeter Waves 2 Hours
MODULE 2 2 Hours
Transmission Lines for Microwave and Millimeter Waves- Microstrip, Suspended
Microstrip, Suspended Stripline, Fin-lines, Dielectric Integrated Guides
MODULE 3 6 Hours
Microwave and Millimeter wave Switches
P-i-n diode switches: basic configurations, Insertion loss and isolation of series and shunt
switches, Series and shunt switches in microstrip, Device reactance compensation,
Isolation improvement techniques, SPDT switches, Application of p-i-n diode switches,
Design Examples
MODULE 4 12 Hours
Microwave and Millimeter Wave Phase Shifters
Analog versus digital Phase Shifters, Principle of ferrite Phase Shift

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 22 -->
Digital Image Processing
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
Equip students with an understanding of digital image processing: image enhancement,
color image analysis, segmentation.
Assessment Criterion
1)Laboratory : 30 %
2) Examination : 50 %
3) Course Work : 20 %
MODULE 1 4 Hours
Digital image Fundamentals:
Elements of Visual Perception, Light and the Electromagnetic Spectrum, Image Sensing
and Acquisition, Image Sampling and Quantization, Some Basic Relationships between
Pixels, Linear and Nonlinear Operations.
MODULE 2 8 Hours
Image Enhancement in spatial domain:
Basic Gray Level Transformations, Histogram Processing, Basics of Spatial Filtering,

Test

Satellite Communication
3-0-0
Learning Outcomes:
This course aims at providing thorough information of the conventional and upcoming satellite
communication technology. The course covers the fundamental aspects of the satellite
communications like orbital theory and link budget analysis. The course would be taught from
physical layer signal processing point‐of‐view and it would cover teaching of famous channel
model of the land mobile satellite (LMS) links and also some recently discovered channel models.
The multiple antennas based satellite communication would also be explained. The hybrid
satellite‐terrestrial satellite communication technology would also be introduced.

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech. With Specialization In “Computer Technology” By The Departments Of Electrical, And Computer Science And Engineering In Ra Scheme Mode

text:
<!-- Page 47 -->
Course Objective:
The objective of this course is to give students a basic grounding in designing and implementing distributed
and cloud systems. Developers of cloud services question how those services should be implemented. This
course will combine hands-on experience in developing cloud services, with a firm grounding in the tools
and principles of building distributed and cloud applications, including advanced architectures such as peer-
to-peer, publish-subscribe and streaming. Besides cloud services, we will also be looking at cloud support
for batch processing, such as the Hadoop and Pig frameworks, and their use with NoSQL data stores such
as Cassandra.
Course Outcome:
Students after completion of the course would be able to:
● Install and Deploy Cloud and Distributed frameworks.
● Create and Destroy VMs for various purposes.
● Execute and Benchmarking.
● Have Hands-on experience with our existing real cloud infrastructure.
● Write and execute the parallel code

11. Course Objective:
The objective of this course is to give students a basic grounding in
designing and implementing distributed and cloud systems. Developers of
cloud services question how those services should be implemented. This
course will combine hands-on experience in developing cloud services, with
a firm grounding in the tools and principles of building distributed and cloud
applications, including advanced architectures such as peer-to-peer, publish-
subscribe and streaming. Besides cloud services, we will also be looking at
cloud support for batch processing, such as the Hadoop and Pig frameworks,
and their use with NoSQL data stores such as Cassandra.
12. Course Outcome:
Students after completion of the course would be able to:
− Install and Deploy Cloud and Distributed frameworks.
− Create and Destroy VMs for various purposes.
− Execute and Benchmarking.
− Have Hands-on experience with our existing real cloud infrastructure.
− Write and execute the paral

| 13. | Course Contents: Introduction to distributed systems and cloud computing. Cloud architectures: SaaS, PaaS, IaaS. End-to-end system design. Networks and protocol stacks, Client-server computing. Sockets and remote procedure call, Distributed file systems and cache consistency. NFS, AFS. Storage in the Cloud: Google/ Hadoop file system. Web services and REST. Example: Amazon S3. The JAX-RS API. Persistent cloud services. Failure models and failure detectors. Asynchrony: publish-subscribe. Server-side events and REST. Web sockets. Vert.x: Node.js for Java. Distributed snapshots. Distributed debugging. Time and ordering of events

Embedded Systems
Syllabus:
The concept of embedded systems design. Architectural Issues: CISC, RISC, DSP Architectures.
Embedded microcontroller cores, embedded memories. Examples of embedded systems.;
Programming in Assembly Language.
Technological aspects of embedded systems: interfacing between analog and digital blocks,
signal conditioning, digital signal processing. sub-system interfacing, interfacing with external
systems, user interfacing.
Designing Embedded Systems : Design Issues, Hardware- Software Co-design, Design trade-
offs due to process compatibility, thermal considerations, etc.;.
Embedded System Software: Program Optimization, Concurrent Programming, Realtime
Scheduling and I/O management;
Real-time concepts, Required RTOS services/capabilities (in contrast with traditional OS)
Resource Management/scheduling paradigms: static priorities, static schedules, dynamic
scheduling, best effort current best practice in scheduling (e.g. Rate Monotonic vs. stat

12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The purpose of this course is to provide students with the skills, knowledge and attitudes
required to determine approximate numerical solutions to mathematical problems which cannot
always be solved by conventional analytical techniques, and to demonstrate the importance of
selecting the right numerical technique for a particular application, and carefully analysing and
interpreting the results obtained.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of the course, the student should be able to:
Identify initial and boundary value problems and choose appropriate numerical schemes.
Apply appropriate algorithms to solve selecte

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech In Mechanical Engineering With Specialization In Thermal And Energy Systems Engineering (Revised 2025)

text:
| 13. | Course outcomes (about 100 words): |
| --- | --- |
| | At the end of the course, the student should be able to: |
| | Identify initial and boundary value problems and choose appropriate numerical schemes. |
| | Apply appropriate algorithms to solve selected problems, both manually and by writing |
| | computer programs. |
| | Analyse the errors obtained in the numerical solution of problems. |
| | Using appropriate numerical methods, determine approximate solutions to systems of linea |
| | equations and ordinary differential equations. |

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M. Tech In Mechanical Engineering With Specialization In Thermal And Energy Systems Engineering (Revised 2025)

text:
<!-- Page 13 -->
14. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet)
(Include Practical / Practice activities):
Approximation and round off errors (Truncation error, absolute error), Root finding method for
non-linear equation i.e. Bi-section method, Secant method, Newton Raphson method (for higher
dimensions also) Review of Matrix inversion techniques. System of Linear Equation: Direct
method - Gauss Elimination, Pivoting etc. LU Factorization, Cholesky method with order of
Convergence and error analysis, Iterative method - Gauss Jacobi Method, Gauss Siedel with
order of Convergence and error analysis. Interpolation Theory: Langrage Interpolation with
Error Analysis, Newton Divided difference interpolation, backward and forward interpolation,
Central interpolation, errro estimate, Hermite interpolation, Piecewise interpolation; Spline:
Quadratic spline, Cubic spline with error analysis. Least Squares method, Weirstrass Theorme,
Gram Schmit proces

text:

11. Will the course require any faculty to be associated
with?
12. Course objectives: (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The aim of the course is to introduce the unified approach to deal with transport phenomena in
energy and momentum transfer applications. Starting from the Reynold transport theorem how
one can select the integral and differential approaches to analyze the real life applications will
be demonstrated.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
At the end of this course, the students will be able to appreciate the different needs of Eulerian
(CV) and Lagrangian (CM) approaches. Depending of the problem in hand, one would be able to
choose a proper system or control volume and select the integral/differential approaches to
identify the governing equations and find the solution strategy.
14. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet)
(Include Practical / Practice activities):
Reynolds transport
