11. Will the course require any faculty to be associated
with?
12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Formation, at a medium level, in heat transfer by conduction, convection and radiation and fluid
dynamics: phenomenological aspects, mathematical formulation (basic conservation laws and
constitutive laws) and analytical and numerical resolution techniques. Methodologies of
resolution of problems of technological interest which different forms of combined heat transfer
are presented.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Basic concepts about momentum, heat and mass transfer. Introduction to general conservation
equations for transport phenomena.
Ability to improve mathematical models and use analytical methods for solving these
mathematical models for transport phenomena.
14. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet)
(Include Practical / Practice activities):

12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The main focus of this laboratory is to make students aware of the various energy sources, their
conversion and utilization. Besides these, the message of environmental sustainability is also
conveyed through these experiments. This will in turn give them a holistic idea of improving
technologies through innovation and thus cater to the development of an energy efficient society
by addressing global issues.
13. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
This laboratory is mainly focused to make the students to understand the basic concepts involved
in energy conversion, solar thermal systems, i.e, Flat plate collector, concentrator and solar PV
systems, wind energy systems.

text: <!-- Page 2 --> Prelude: Information-centric digital society needs to exploit data to bring in (i) transparency, (ii) fair- ness, (iii) accuracy, and (iv) con(cid:28)dentiality in transforming unstructured data to information (and knowledge) for information mining and decision-making. With the increasing volume of data, organizations are getting more and more interested in unravelling the unintuitive trends that lay hidden within the data. These trends can be connected with valuable business in- sights that are usable in every department, be it marketing or product development or design. Evidence-based decision making has established itself. Various levels in business have to answer di(cid:27)erent questions as their company evolves, identify common threads across a multitude of similar questions and the right tools for the right question. Data science is used in business functions such as strategy formation, operational processes, and decision making. It is important to understand tha

To address the mounting need of high-skilled professionals in these two areas, IIT Jammu and DA-IICT, Gandhinagar with patronage from the RC Bose center, ISI, Kolkata are proud to announce a new postgraduate program - MTech in Computer Science with Specialization in (i) Data Science (CS-DS), and (ii) Information Security (CS-IS). The degree would be conferred by IIT-Jammu while students spend their study semester across the three institutes, depending upon how their research interests align with the research expertise at these three institutes. Speci(cid:28)cally, the students are expected to study their (cid:28)rst semester at IIT Jammu, the second at DA-IICT, Gandhinagar. The students will be exposed to various categories of data and management tools. They will become familiar with techniques using real-time and semi-structured data examples. Using Machine Learning algorithms students would be able to learn techniques of mining high-velocity and high-volume data. text: <!-- Page 31 --> 18 Security and Privacy Issues in Resource Constrained Environments: This course is concerned with study of the security issues in the resources constrained en- vironments viz. the Wireless Sensor Networks, RFID Systems, Mobile Adhoc Networks and the Internet of Things. While topics such as cryptography, networking, network protocols are touched upon, this is not a course on those topics. Instead, this course is on understanding the characteristics of the Wireless Sensor Networks, the RFID Systems, and the Internet of Things; identi(cid:28)cation of potential threats and vulnerabilities in such environments and techniques for mitigating such threats. The emphasis in this course is on the design of the security protocols to ensure protection against the security attacks, and ways to avoid security holes in Wireless Sensor Networks, RFID Systems, and the Internet of Things. Department Vision:
“To be an epitome of excellence in chemical engineering science with world class

Department Mission:
The mission of

text:
<!-- Page 3 -->
required to successfully complete the research component of the program which starts
from third semester onwards. The PG program may lead to a M. Tech degree in
Chemical Engineering with specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering
(SEE) (starting in the academic year 2023-24). The specialization component for the
degree will be completed by selecting the appropriate number of elective courses in the
given specialization from the approved list of courses offered by the department. In case
any guidance is required by the student for the choice of electives they may approach
the respective supervisor.
In the second year of the program, the student will involve themselves in research
projects under the supervision of a faculty member. Here, the student will have to do an
MTech Project related to specialization. If the student at any point feels they would not
like to specialize in Sustainable Energy Engineering they may choose a project in
general chemical engineering

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Mtech In Chemical Engineering With Specialization In Sustainable Energy Engineering

text:
<!-- Page 4 -->
Specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering (SEE)
The main objective of the specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering (SEE) is to
prepare the students for:
• Carry out research in energy systems such as renewable energy (Solar, Hydrogen),
electrochemical energy conversion systems (fuel cells, batteries and miniaturized
devices), heat transfer, energy intensive materials, hydrogen energy, kinetics and
molecular modelling,green chemical process, energy economics.
• Getting employment in public/private industrial energy sectors, Government energy
agencies, consultancy energy services and educational institution dealing with energy.
• Pursue for higher studies /employment in nationaland international institutions working
on energy and environment.
• Successfully carry out their profession as an entrepreneur and team player with
innovative approach to provide economical and sustainable solutions to solve energy
challenges for the overall societal progress.

At the end of this course, Masters and PhD students are expected to
- Formulate and manipulate phase equilibrium problems for complex systems basedon classical
and molecular thermodynamics.
- Analyze specific chemical engineering problems using fundamental concepts.
- To relate thermodynamics, processes with practical problems and provide innovative solutions.
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include
Practical / Practice activities): Reviews of the laws of thermodynamics; molecular theory and
intermolecular forces; equilibrium and stability; phase equilibrium; problem formulation and
applications in chemical processes, biochemical, electrolyte and non-electrolyte solutions and
fuel cells; irreversible thermodynamics; phase transformations

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The course aims to
• Learn a few basic numerical techniques (Finite Difference) to solve simple
ODEs/PDEs/algebraic equations derived from a variety of heat and mass transfer related
conservation equations.
• Analytical methods yield exact solutions, often in infinite series; use mathematical
functions.
• Numerical methods yield approximate solutions; use numbers, computations; results are
dependent on the method accuracies and limited by computers machine errors.
• To learn the basics of MATLAB/Python programing and to write simple MATLAB code.

# M Tech in Chemical Engineering with specialization in Sustainable Energy Revised Curriculum 2023 onwards

<!-- Page 2 -->
Mission and Vision
Department Vision:
“To be an epitome of excellence in chemical engineering science with world class
multidisciplinary approach towards sustained growth in academics and research with
impe

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M Tech In Chemical Engineering With Specialization In Sustainable Energy Revised Curriculum 2023 Onwards

text:
<!-- Page 3 -->
departmental courses bolster the fundamental and advanced concepts of chemical
engineering in a holistic manner which includes reaction engineering, transport
phenomena, thermodynamics as well as numerical and statistical methods . The core
courses will help students assimilate the necessary skills required to successfully
complete the research component of the program which starts from third semester
onwards. The PG program may lead to a M. Tech degree in Chemical Engineering
with specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering (SEE) (starting in the
academic year 2023-24). The specialization component for the degree will be
completed by selecting the appropriate number of elective courses in the given
specialization from the approved list of courses offered by the department. In case any
guidance is required by the student for the choice of electives they may approach the
respective supervisor.
In the second year of the program, the student will involve themselves in

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech M Tech In Chemical Engineering With Specialization In Sustainable Energy Revised Curriculum 2023 Onwards

text:
<!-- Page 4 -->
Specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering (SEE)
The main objective of the specialization in Sustainable Energy Engineering (SEE) is to
prepare the students for:
• Carry out research in energy systems such as renewable energy (Solar, Hydrogen),
electrochemical energy conversion systems (fuel cells, batteries and miniaturized
devices), heat transfer, energy intensive materials, hydrogen energy, kinetics and
molecular modelling , green chemical process, energy economics.
• Getting employment in public/private industrial energy sectors, Government energy
agencies, consultancy energy services and educational institution dealing with
energy.
• Pursue for higher studies /employment in national and international institutions
working on energy and environment.
• Successfully carry out their profession as an entrepreneur and team player with
innovative approach to provide economical and sustainable solutions to solve energy
challenges for the overall societal progress.
Sem

To course aims to
• Provide students with the concepts, and laws of classical thermodynamics to applications
that require quantitative knowledge of thermodynamic properties for different
applications of Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics.
• To develop understanding of thermodynamic processes occurring in Chemical
Engineering.
• To understand multi-component interactions, practical examples and industrial
applications.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words) :
At the end of this course, Masters and PhD students are expected to
- Formulate and manipulate phase equilibrium problems for complex systems based on classical
and molecular thermodynamics.
- Analyze specific chemical engineering problems using fundamental concepts.
- To relate thermodynamics, proc

12. Course Outcome:
After completion of the course the students will be able to
• Appreciate the analogy between momentum, energy and mass transport.
• Apply boundary conditions for momentum, heat, and mass transport
13 Course Contents:
. • Review of basic mathematical tools (vector calculus, theorems of integral calculus)
(2 lecture)
• Kinematics- Continuum hypothesis, Reference frames (Lagrangian vs Eulerian)
(1 lecture)
• General property balance equations- Continuity, Conservation of linear momentum,
application to angular momentum, energy conservation equation, and equation of change
for species, dimensional analysis. (5 lectures)
• Momentum transport in laminar flow- (4 lectures)
• Rayleigh Problem or Stoke’s First Problem
• Unsteady-state (start-up) flow in a tube
• Heat (and Mass) transfer in laminar flow- (8 lectures)
• Gratz-Nusselt Problem
• Gratz Problem
• Brinkman Problem
• Natural convection: confined flow
• Mass transfer in laminar flow- (6 lectures)
• S

(5 lectures) • Momentum transport in laminar flow- (4 lectures) • Rayleigh Problem or Stoke’s First Problem • Unsteady-state (start-up) flow in a tube • Heat (and Mass) transfer in laminar flow- (8 lectures) • Gratz-Nusselt Problem • Gratz Problem • Brinkman Problem • Natural convection: confined flow • Mass transfer in laminar flow- (6 lectures) • Steady-state diffusion through a stagnant gas film (Film Theory) • Diffusion into a falling film (Surface renewal theory) • Taylor-Aris dispersion in a laminar flow in a tube • Condensation of steam in the presence of an inert gas

1. Introduction to chemical reaction engineering: 2 Lectures
2. Non-ideal reactors: macro and micro mixing, residence time distributions, design of
non-ideal reactors: segregated and non-segregated models (mixing cells and dispersion
models). 8 Lectures
3. Heterogeneous reactions: kinetics and mechanism of catalytic reactions, adsorption,
isotherms, Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism, characterization of solid catalysts
(BET area, pore size distribution, pore diffusion, porosity), observed and intrinsic
reaction rates. 8 Lectures
4. Non-porous catalyst reaction: isothermal and non-isothermal reactions, Damkohler
number, effectiveness factor, Prater number, interphase diffusion and reaction control
6 Lectures
5. Porous catalyst reaction: isothermal and non-isothermal reactions, Thiele modulus,
effectiveness factor, intraphase diffusion and reaction control 8 Lectures
6. Interphase, intraphase and reaction controlled reactions: rules and criterion 2 Lect

Introduction, Thermochemistry: stoichiometry, first and second law of thermodynamics
applied to combustion, heat of reaction, adiabatic flame temperature, chemical equilibrium,
Chemical kinetics: definition of rate of reaction, relative rate of reactions, rate law (law of
mass action), reaction order and molecularity, single (global) and multiple reactions,
elementary and non-elementary reactions, reversible reactions, rate of reaction for multistep
mechanism, chain and chain branching reactions, criteria for explosion, general oxidative
characteristics of fuel, steady-state approximation, partial equilibrium, Coupling chemical
and thermal analyses of reacting systems : constant-pressure fixed mass reactor,
constant-volume fixed mass reactor, well-stirred reactor, plug-flow reactor, reactor network,
applications to combustion system modeling, Laminar premixed flames: Introduction to
deflagration and detonation waves, physical description, flam

13. Course Contents:
The course covers the concept of ecological balance and the contribution of
industrial and human activities in the changes of the environmental quality.
Ecological cycles. Concept of pollutants and regulatory measures for the
maintenance of environmental quality. Air pollution sources and its dependence on
the atmospheric factors, atmospheric stability and dispersion of pollutants. Control
of emission of pollutants using multi-cyclone systems, electrostatic precipitators, bag
filters, wet scrubbers for gas cleaning, adsorption by activated carbon etc. Water
pollution, its causes and effects. Pollutants and its dispersion in water bodies to
predict water quality through modelling. Concept of inorganic and organic wastes
and definition of BOD and COD. Control of water pollution by primary treatment and
biological treatment systems. Solid waste management systems. Hazardous waste
tr

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
• To gain the knowledge of design of tunnels
• To gain the various support systems for tunnels and their design
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Upon successful completion, the students will be
- Able to understand the concepts of tunnel design and will be able to design a tunnel
- Able to decide the type of supporting system and design the system
13. Course contents: (Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities): Introduction; Tunnel requirements - operational, structural; Surveying and
alignment in tunnels; Choice of tunnel system, shape and size of cross section, rock pressure
and cavity stabilization; Properties and types of supports, design of support systems, tunnel
lining.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?)
(about 100 words): - To learn various geological
features of earth
- To know the types of rocks and their formation,
classification
- To know the engineering properties of rocks
- To know the failure theories of rock, rock mass
classification theories
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After successful completion of course the students will be able to
- Know how the rocks form
- Know the properties of rock and how to measure them
- Know the rock mass classification and failure theories

/ Practice activities): Geology and its importance, Mineralogy, Classification of rocks - igneous rocks,

geology, engineering properties of rocks, Introduction to rock me

- To learn the various tunnel excavation methods
- To learn various tunnel excavation philosophies

After successful completion, the student will be able to
- Understand various tunneling methods and their advantages and uses
- Understand various tunneling philosophies and their application based on soil type

Practice activities):
Tunnel Excavation Methods, Tunneling in Rocks, Tunneling in Soft Ground; Tunneling
Philosophies, Ground (Rock) Support Interaction, New Austrian Tunneling Method, Norwegian
Method of Tunneling, ADECO-RS Method of Tunneling

theoretical and computational aspects of fracture for students interested in exploring the
analysis of fracture based failures

1. use fracture mechanics in design and analysis of flawed components

numerically.

- To study and understand provisions in different types of contracts.
- To interpret from the various contract conditions.
- To analyze legal aspects involved in contracts.

- Differentiate between various contract types and select the appropriate type needed for a
particular project.
- Scrutinize contractor’s bids and select a reliable, competent contractor.
- Analyze the provisions made in the Contract Act and Arbitration act; interpret their
repercussions on the contract performance.
- Apply the FIDIC document provisions in contracting practice.

Courses of Study : Syllabus, Course Abstracts and
Course Outcomes
1 Linear Algebra:
This course is about teaching basics of linear algebra including vector spaces, matrix algebra,
linear transformations, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, orthogonality, properties of symmetric ma-
trices, positive definite matrices, and SVD. The course is developed with an aim to provide a
strong foundation in linear algebra which will be used in the subsequent curriculum by both the
specialization of DS and IS students. The course also attempts to increase the mathematical
maturity of students by introducing proofs and mathematical rigour.
IndicativeTopicsofStudy: Determinants-rowreductionandcofactorexpansions,Cramer’s
rule. Rowpicture, Columnpicture, VectorSpaces-Euclideanspace, general(real)vectorspaces,
subspaces, linear independence, dimension, row, column and null spaces.
Innerproducts: norms,orthogonalbasesandGram-Schmidtorthogonalization; MatrixMultipli-
cation Problems, Matrix Analy

text: <!-- Page 8 --> • Communicate and understand mathematical statements, ideas and results, both verbally and in writing, with the correct use of mathematical definitions, terminology and symbol- ism (Communication Skills). 2 Probability and Statistics: This course is about enabling the learner to acquire knowledge required to build algorithms for machine learning, data science and information security. A student must have knowledge about Bayes theorem (conditional probability), random variable, discrete and continuous distribution, Joint distribution function, marginal and conditional distributions, expectation, covariance, law of large numbers, central limit theorem. Indicative Topics of Study: Introduction to Probability, conditional Probability, Bayes’ theorem; Random variables, Analysis of discrete and continuous random variables. Probability distributions, distribution functions, mean and variance of random variables , standard discrete and continuous distributions and their propert

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Master Of Technology In Computer Science And Engineering (Revised 2023)

text: data using probability distribution • Analyze the dependence of random variables in the sense of correlation and regression • Understand data parameter estimation and test of hypotheses. 3 Design and Analysis of Algorithms: In this course, the students would learn about the design and the analysis of the algorithms. Algorithms are the soul of computing. Algorithmic thinking is akin to creating "recipes" (well defined sequences of computational steps) for getting "things" (computational problems speci- fying an input-output relation) “successfully" (correctly) "done" (in finite steps and time). This course introduces basic methods for the design and analysis of efficient algorithms emphasiz- ing methods useful in practice. Different algorithms for a given computational task would be presented and their relative merits evaluated based on performance measures. The following 7

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Master Of Technology In Computer Science And Engineering (Revised 2023)

text: <!-- Page 9 --> important computational problems will be discussed: sorting, searching, elements of dynamic programmingandgreedyalgorithms,advanceddatastructures,graphalgorithms(shortestpath, spanning trees, tree traversals), string matching, elements of computational geometry, NP com- pleteness. Indicative Topics of Study: Review of Basis concepts in Algorithms, Abstract Machines, Analysis Techniques: Mathematical, Empirical and Asymptotic analysis, Review of the nota- tions in asymptotic analysis, Recurrence Relations and Solving Recurrences, Proof Techniques - Illustrations. Divide and Conquer approach - Review of Sorting & order statistics. Various Comparison based Sorts. Analysis. Medians and Order Statistics. The Union-Find problem, Counting Inversions - Finding the closest pair of points. Lower Bound on Sorting and Non-comparison based sorts. Searching and set manipulation: Searching in static table binary search, path lengths in binary trees and applications. Optimality of bina

text: algorithm and its analysis. Probabilistic Algorithms. Motivation. Probabilistic Analysis - Indicator Random Variables - Four main design categories - Randomization of deterministic algorithms - Monte Carlo Al- gorithms - Las Vegas Algorithms - Numerical Probabilistic Algorithms & Various candidate applications therein. Approximation Algorithms - Introduction and Motivation for Approximation Algorithms - Greedy and combinatorial methods. Scheduling: multiprocessor scheduling. NP-Theory & Selected Topics - Polynomial time verification - NP-completeness & the Search Problems - The reductions - Dealing with NP-completeness - Local Search Heuristics - Space complexity. Selected topics - Algorithms for String Matching - Amortized Analysis - Bloom Filters & their applications References: 1. text: an operating system and the network protocols that a modern computer system invariably uses. The course is aimed to serve as a foundation for courses on information security, compilers, networks, operating systems, and computer architecture, where a deeper understanding of systems-level issues is required. The course is aimed to make a student become more effective programmer, especially in dealing with issues of performance, portability and robustness. The topics covered include: machine-level codeanditsgenerationbyoptimizingcompilers, performanceevaluationandoptimization, com- puter arithmetic, memory organization and management, operating system and its functional components,networkingtechnologyandprotocols,andsupportingconcurrentcomputation. Hence, the artifacts that come into play at each stage of SDLC and the tools and techniques used to design and build such artifacts will be taught. In addition, a student would beexposedhowtomodelsecurityattacksusingformalapproaches, designthemisuseandab-use case,ascomparedtotheusecasesandthenidentifythesecurityrequirementsandthedesignthe software to prevent the attacks. text: <!-- Page 20 --> 5 Security Protocols: Protocols describe how communication between entities takes place. It is commonly believed that a security protocol may fail in three ways: the protocol design may be flawed, the cryp- tographic primitives used in the protocol may be weak, or the implementation contains bugs. This course aims to covers security properties, threats, vulnerabilities and attacks pertaining to various applications ranging from enterprise applications to electronic cash. The course pro- vides substantial exposure on design and analysis aspects of security protocols such as classical key establishment protocols, electronic voting, multi-party computing, and fair exchange with emphasis on formal security analysis approach. Course Outcomes: Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to do the following: • Understand, practice and learn the fundamental toolboxes for designing and analyzing existing security protocols. • Be able to analyze, design a

text: <!-- Page 25 --> 18 Bigdata and Large-Scale Computing: In today’s era of Big Data Analytics and Mining, where we are constantly faced with high- velocity and high-volume streaming data, this course is designed to provide an introduction to some of the most common frameworks, including Hadoop and Spark, that have made big data analysis easier. The students will be exposed to various categories of data and management tools. They will become familiar with techniques using real-time and semi-structured data examples. The course will also provide techniques to extract value from existing data sources and discovering new data sources. Using Machine Learning algorithms students would be able to learn techniques of mining high-velocity and high-volume data. In this regard, students will be introduced to tools and scalable algorithms. Indicative Topics of Study: Introduction. Hadoop Fundamentals. Data Logistics, Import- ing and Exporting Data. Working with Hive and Pig Hadoop Use Cases. Big Dat

text: <!-- Page 26 --> 19 Optimization Techniques: The main purpose of this course is to provide the students the working knowledge of several optimization techniques and numerical approaches to solve optimization problems in Data Sci- ence. The course contents the linear and nonlinear convex optimization problems, and their analytical solution methods. It also discusses some numerical optimization techniques in partic- ular, gradient method, gradient descent method, steepest descent methods, Newton’s method, proximal methods etc. that are useful to solve optimization problems aroused from Data science and Machine learning. It also discusses the analysis of these methods and sensitivity of these methods towards variation of data and parameters. text: <!-- Page 20 --> 5 Security Protocols: Protocols describe how communication between entities takes place. It is commonly believed that a security protocol may fail in three ways: the protocol design may be flawed, the cryp- tographic primitives used in the protocol may be weak, or the implementation contains bugs. This course aims to covers security properties, threats, vulnerabilities and attacks pertaining to various applications ranging from enterprise applications to electronic cash. The course pro- vides substantial exposure on design and analysis aspects of security protocols such as classical key establishment protocols, electronic voting, multi-party computing, and fair exchange with emphasis on formal security analysis approach. Aftersurveyingbasiccryptographic techniques, the course examines several secure networking protocols, including PGP, SSL, IPsec and wireless security protocols. The course examines operational security, including firewalls and intrusion-detection

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Master Of Technology In Computer Science And Engineering (Three Years)

text: <!-- Page 27 --> Course Outcomes: Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to do the following: • Evaluate and recognize a problem as being a possible network security threat. • Analyze the network traffic to identify different types of Denial of Service attacks. • Design a firewall for a typical network setup, including specifying the rules for filtering traffic. • Design an intrusion detection system. • Analyze the security strength of a typical installation using the TLS and/or IPSec proto- cols. • Design a security protocol that works at either the Application layer, Transport layer or the Network layer. • Analyze the security issues in Network operating systems, Web applications and the Web browsers. 22 Security and Privacy Issues in Resource Constrained Environments: This course is concerned with study of the security issues in the resources constrained en- vironments viz. the Wireless Sensor Networks, RFID Systems, Mobile Adhoc Networks and the Interne

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Master Of Technology In Computer Science And Engineering (Three Years)

text: as well as hands on experimentations. • Design a hop-by-hop link layer security architecture and end-to-end security architecture for the Wireless Sensor Networks and the Internet of Things systems. • DesignasecurityprotocolusingHomomorphicEncryptionCiphersandofAttributeBased Cryptography for the embedded systems. • Understand the design of existing ciphers for resource constrained devices and for embed- ded systems. • Understandthesecurityprovisionsinthetypicaloperatingsystemsforembeddedsystems. • Analyze the security protocol stack of a typical Internet of Things architecture. 23 Application Security: In this course the learner will be taught the design and implementation of secure applications. The emphasis would be exposing the learner to the techniques of writing software programs that make it difficult for intruders to exploit security holes. The students would be able to learn the fundamental technical skills required to identify and prevent application vulnerabilities. 26

metadata.title: Curriculum Pg Mtech Master Of Technology In Computer Science And Engineering (Three Years)

text:
---

<!-- Page 28 -->
The course emphasizes writing secure programs in C and secure distributed programs in object
oriented Java. The security ramifications of class, field and method visibility are emphasized as
applicable.
Course Outcomes: Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to
do the following:
• Learn about the tools they will need to understand and assess software security.
• Learn how to apply the theory and practice of code auditing.
• Analyze/dissect an application, discover security vulnerabilities, and assess the danger
each vulnerability presents.
• Understand and apply issues of secure programming principles i.e. different variable nam-
ing styles, code review processes, managing security bug bounties, unit testing philosophy,
and how to write effective documentation.
• Design and implement various live projects, including a 6 week project where students
would take an existing large code base and write a substantial new piece of functionality

Assignments 2: [week 3]
You have write a script which crawl and extract the data from different websites.
You are also required to pre-process and analyze this data using Weka tool.
Assignments 3: [week 4]
You are required to develop a profiler for a program in C/C++/Python. The
profiler must be able to check different amount of space utilized for an executed
program like stack memory, heap memory, segmentation faults, memory leak,
dangling pointer, execution time, process information etc.
Assignments 4: [week 5]
Group project: You have to create a GUI for file format conversion (like txt to
docs, csv to rtf and many more). At least 10 different extensions of file should be
implemented.

can decrypt an encrypted message. The System makes use of the SMS that you

is by default encrypted in the message. So whenever the user is sending a message
he should know the receiver’s id as the id is also appended to the message so that
while the receiver logins to the system the message is already decrypted if he is

users ease the system allows the user to save the recipient’s id in a separate

here as a single user can have multiple accounts with different ids so that he can

This System makes use of AES Encryp

# Minor in Economics

## Department of Humanities and Social Sciences

---

# Programme Overview

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences proposes a **Minor in Economics** programme aimed at introducing students to both foundational and specialized topics in economics and related disciplines.

The programme is designed to build upon the concepts introduced in the core course:

> **Introduction to Economics**

and extend them into advanced theoretical, analytical, and application-oriented domains.

The curriculum combines:

- Quantitative approaches
- Qualitative approaches
- Policy analysis
- Economic theory
- Data-driven methods
- Entrepreneu

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor Economics

text:
## Data-Oriented and Applied Courses

These courses emphasize empirical analysis and practical applications.

Examples include:

- Basic Econometrics
- Econometrics for Business and Public Policy

Students will learn how to:

- Work with real-world datasets.
- Interpret economic indicators.
- Analyze policy outcomes.
- Understand interactions among sectors of the economy.

---

## Development and Policy Perspective

Courses focused on:

- Indian Economy
- Development Economics
- International Trade

provide insight into:

- Developing economies
- Economic growth
- Global economic interactions
- India's economic landscape

---

# Benefits to Students

The programme is expected to provide significant academic and professional advantages.

## Broader Knowledge Base

Students will gain exposure beyond traditional engineering disciplines through a deeper understanding of:

- Economics
- Public policy
- Business
- Society

---

## Enhanced Career Opportunities

Knowledge of economics can ope

---

# Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of the Minor in Economics, students will be able to:

- Apply economic reasoning to real-world problems.
- Analyze markets and institutions.
- Understand public policy implications.
- Work with economic and business datasets.
- Evaluate technology from economic and societal perspectives.
- Develop entrepreneurial and strategic thinking skills.
- Understand interactions among technology, economy, and society.

<!-- Page 1 -->
Proposal of Minor in Mathematics for UG students
The Department of Mathematics, IIT Jammu plans to offer a program ‘Minor in Mathematics’
for UG students in which they are expected to build strong mathematical foundations. It is well-
known that mathematical methods are instrumental today in numerous career fields including
(but not limited to) medicine, law, design, engineering, finance, accounting, economics,
architecture, and computer programming. Mathematics is the backbone of several engineering
concepts. However, it is quite difficult to provide the detailed underlying mathematical
concepts during the courses (engineering) due to time constraints. The introduction of a minor
program in Mathematics in the U.G. The curriculum is projected to set a platform for
understanding those mathematical concepts.
1. Objectives of the program
The minor program in Mathematics in an academic curriculum is to facilitate our
students to appreciate the relevance of Mathematics assoc

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor Mathematics (2019 Scheme)

text:
<!-- Page 8 -->
Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to do the
following:
● can work with sets and functions, images and preimages, and you can
distinguish between finite, countable, and uncountable sets;
● know how the topology on a space is determined by the collection of open sets,
by the collection of closed sets, or by a basis of neighbourhoods at each point,
and you know what it means for a function to be continuous;
● know the definition and basic properties of connected spaces, path connected
spaces, compact spaces, and locally compact spaces;
● know what it means for a metric space to be complete, and you can characterize
compact metric spaces;
● are familiar with the Urysohn lemma and the Tietze extension theorem, and you
can characterize metrizable spaces;
● are familiar with the construction of the fundamental group of a topological space
and applications to covering spaces and homotopy theory.
13. Course Contents:
Topological Spaces: open set

text:
<!-- Page 12 -->
12. Course Outcome:
Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to do the
following:
● Demonstrate understanding of common numerical methods and how they are
used to obtain approximate solutions to otherwise intractable mathematical
problems.
● Apply numerical methods to obtain approximate solutions to mathematical
problems.
● Derive numerical methods for various mathematical operations and tasks, such as
interpolation, differentiation, integration, the solution of linear and nonlinear
equations, and the solution of differential equations.
● Analyse and evaluate the accuracy of common numerical methods.
● Write efficient, well-documented Matlab code and present numerical results in an
informative way.
13. Course Contents:
Error analysis: source and types of errors, floating-Point approximation of a
number, floating point arithmetic, loss of significance and error propagation, stability
in numerical computation.
Linear systems: direct and iterati

structures.

● understand abstractly about mathematical structures
● recognize and comprehend correct proofs of formal statements.

12. Course Outcome:
Students who complete the course will have demonstrated the ability to do the
following:
i) Inverse and Implicit function theorems and applications.
ii) Local and global properties of plane and space curves.
iii) Surfaces in Euclidean space, tangent and normal spaces, curvature of surfaces,
Geodesics, 1st and 2nd fundamental form, Minimal surfaces.
iv) Integration on surfaces, Gauss Bonnet theorem and applications, Euler
characteristics. Divergence theorem and applications.
13. Course Contents:
(i) Review of differentiability for functions of several real variables, Chain rule, mean
value theorem.
(ii) Inverse and Implicit function theorems, the rank theorem and applications.
(iii) Curves: Plane and space curves, local and global properties of curves.
(iv) Surfaces in 3-dimension, tangent and normal spaces, Curvature of surfaces,
Gauss Map, First and second fundamental forms, Gauss's theorema Egregium,

• Arithmetic Functions and their properties

13. Course Contents:
• Divisibility: basic definition, properties, prime numbers, some results on
distribution of primes.
• Congruences: basic definitions and properties, complete and reduced residue
systems, theorems of Fermat, Euler & Wilson, application to RSA cryptosystem,
linear congruences and Chinese Remainder theorem, quadratic congruences,
and Quadratic Reciprocity law.
• Arithmetical functions: examples, with some properties and their rate of growth.
• Continued fractions, and their connections with Diophantine approximations,
applications to linear and Pell’s equations; Binary quadratic forms.
• Partition: basic properties and results;
• Diophantine equations: linear and quadratic, some general equations.

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The theory of differential equations plays an important role in understanding the laws of science
and technology. Differential Equations arise as a mathematical model for many exciting real-
life problems, not only in science and technology, but also in such diverse fields as economics,
psychology, defense, and demography. For example, the motion of a vibrating string is
modeled by a one dimensional wave equation, measuring the quantity of heat diffuses through
a given region is modeled by the heat equation etc. Due to the potential applications in the
various fields of science and technology, the theory of differential equations is very important
in our daily life. Theory of differential equations consists of the existence, uniqueness and
stability issue for the solution to the differential equations.

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor Nuclearengineering-Iit Jammu 27Feb2020

text: <!-- Page 1 --> Proposal for Minor in Nuclear Engineering The introduction of minor program in the U.G. curriculum is expected to provide a platform to launch an independent nuclear research and development program at IIT Jammu. The R&D unit at IIT Jammu will act as an extended academic and research wing supplementing the efforts of Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) of India and pave way for an additional route towards flow of information related to the state of the art in nuclear technology, from abroad to India and vice-versa through international academic collaborations and exchange of visiting scientists and academicians. 1. Objectives of the minor program in nuclear engineering The minor program in nuclear engineering in an academic curriculum is to facilitate our students to appreciate the relevance of nuclear energy globally and in India, and the associated technological challenges. Further, the concurrently planned research program being initiated by IIT Jammu with the agencies

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor Nuclearengineering-Iit Jammu 27Feb2020

text: ongoing R&D efforts to develop the Generation-IV reactors by several countries known as the Generation-IV International Forum. To realise India’s unique three stage nuclear power program, self-reliant and independent development of the nuclear technology is required. It demands extensive and close research collaborations between DAE units of India and academia. IIT Jammu may take a lead role in establishing such industry-academia collaborations and continue long term research association. IIT Jammu’s initiative to start a minor would help our students and faculty to be part of this initiative and would make them aware of the developmental issues involved and prepare them to take up similar challenges in other engineering fields as well. b. Prospective employability and opportunities for higher studies
Different wings of the DAE as well as different organisations connected with nuclear power,
such as Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd. (NPCIL), Bharatiya Nabhikiya Vidyut Nigam
Limited (BHAVINI), Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Centre for Advanced Technology
(CAT) Indore, Indira Gandhi Centre for Atomic Research (IGCAR), Kalpakkam, etc., offer about
100 scientific officer/engineer’s position every year for good undergraduate students. These
jobs are superior to that offered by the public sector companies as they offer comparable or
better salaries along with good technological and analytical content in their work
environment. A good foundation in nuclear engineering will put our undergraduates at a
relatively advantageous position when they will be competing with others. Theoretical and
practical understanding in the nuclear engineering courses along with the possibility of

12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The course aims to provide the basic understanding of fundamentals of reactor physics design of a fission
reactor. In addition, the course will give enough design insights on the control and safety of fission chain
reaction.
13. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
The course would provide a detailed understanding of the neutronics required for sustaining nuclear
chain reaction in a nuclear reactor. At the end of the course, the students would be capable of
performing elementary neutronic design calculations. They would also be able to appreciate the reactor
safety issues associated with nuclear reactor operation.
14. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities):
Relevance of nuclear engineering, Nuclear reactor systems, Nuclear reactions, radioactive decay,
decay chains, Fission and fusion reactions, reaction cross

11. Will the course require any faculty to be associated with? Goutam Dutta
12. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To introduce applications of thermal-hydraulic principles in the design and analysis of nuclear reactors
during normal operation, transients and accidents
13. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
On completion of the course, the students will be able to do preliminary steady state design of heat
transport system of a nuclear reactor. They will also be able to do preliminary analysis of these systems
during normal operation, transients and accidents, such as Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA), Loss of Flow
Accident (LOFA), Transient Over Power Accident (TOPA), etc.
14. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities):
Overview of nuclear reactor systems, Heat generation in reactors, Thermal constraints, analysis
of conduction, Heat transfer charact

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
To get hands on experience and visualise the performance of radiation detectors. They will also be able
to experience some of the principles taught in the reactor physics and thermal-hydraulics courses.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
The students will be able to use radiation detectors and will also be able to understand phenomena of
natural circulation and critical heat flux.

transmission, Counting statistics

coefficient in forced and natural circulation, heat transfer in pool boiling, CHF, Steady state and
stability behav

16. Brief description of Practical / Practice activities
Module no. Description No. of
hours
1 Characteristics of a gas filled detector 2
2 Determination of resolving time of a GM (Geiger-Muller) counter 2
3 Measurement of gamma energies using a multi-channel analyser 2

5 Measurement of aluminium foil thickness using β-particle back scattering 2
Measurement of ash content in coal using β-particle back scattering
Measurement of aluminium foil thickness by β-particle transmission
6 Characterization of an ionization chamber 2
7 Measurement of level using γ-ray attenuation technique 2

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Fast reactor technology is required for the long term sustainability of nuclear power. In the Indian
context, fast reactor technology has special significance as this is expected to be the workhorse for the
2nd stage of India’s three-stage nuclear power programme. The fast reactor technology is also crucial for
the take-off of the third stage.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words): This course essentially gives the student an introduction to the fast
breeder reactor, its heat transport systems, and control systems. The student will be able to appreciate
the design considerations for the fast reactor core and its fuel. The student will also get familiar with the
quality control measures to be adopted for the validation of fuel and clad design.
13. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities): Core physics, core de

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
Knowledge of nuclear fuel cycle is important for the entire nuclear power programme. This course will
give the participant an introduction to the complexities involved in nuclear fuel cycle. A good knowledge
of the nuclear fuel cycle is important for an efficient nuclear power programme. Unlike many other
countries, the three-stage nuclear power programme relies on the closed fuel cycle which involves spent
fuel reprocessing which is also covered in this course.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words): A participant to this course will be given a good introduction to the
front end and back end of the nuclear fuel cycle both of which are important for our country’s nuclear
programme. The front end of the nuclear fuel cycle starting with mining, extraction, fuel fabrication and
end with irradiation in power reactors. The back end dealing with spent fuel storage, reprocessing and
waste management is

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The course will introduce the principles of radiation detection and the effect of radiation on living
tissues. It will also introduce the radiation protection standards that are followed internationally.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
On completing the course, the students will have a good knowledge of the amount of radiation that is
permissible. They will be able to get first hand estimates on how long a person can be exposed to a given
dose as admissible by radiation protection standards.
13. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities):
Interaction of radiation with matter (alphas, Betas, Gammas and neutrons) Radiation detection
principles, Gas filled detectors, Scintillation detectors, solid state detectors, Biological effect of radiation,
Cell Biology, Radiation standards, ALARA, Personal monitoring,

11. Course objectives : Nuclear engineering is a multi-disciplinary area. This course essentially describes
the chemical engineer’s role in the nuclear fuel cycle starting with mining of uranium. The participant
will be exposed to the chemical engineering principles applied to extract the various fuel materials,
enrichment of the fuel, and reprocessing of the spent fuel.
12. Course outcomes: The participant would be able to understand the chemical engineering principles
used to produce the fuel from the ore, reprocess the spent fuel and design the various equipment
required to accomplish the task.
13. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities):
Nuclear Chemical Engineering: Ore processing for Uranium, Zirconium and Thorium, Separation of
Nuclear Metals: Solvent Extraction-Purex, Thorex and Urex processes, Isotope Separation- Light
Isotopes: Distillation & Electrolysis, Hydrogen/Deuteri

11. Course objectives: The nuclear reactor components design is significantly different compared to similar
components in other industries especially since nuclear components have to function in radiation
environment and are susceptible to radiation damage and embrittlement. The participants would be
introduced to the basic principles used in nuclear component design as part of this course.
12. Course outcomes: The course participants will get an overview of the design principles involved in the
design of nuclear components. With this, they would be able to accomplish preliminary design of the
reactor systems components.
13. Course contents:(Topics to appear as course contents in the Courses of Study booklet) (Include Practical /
Practice activities):
Design of important reactor components, Radiation damage and its assessment, Materials selection,
Life assessment, Reactor fuel design

11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The course will introduce the principles of degradation, radiation damage and mechanisms,
and, materials and mechanical properties for different reactors.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
On completing the course, the students will have a good knowledge of the material properties
and behaviour due to irradiation in different reactors.

Practical / Practice activities):
Degradation Phenomena & Material limitations in Nuclear Energy & Technology, Radiation Damage,
Crystal defects, Radiation effects & mechanisms, Mechanical Properties, Materials for different reactors

# Minor in Computer Science and Engineering
# Indian Institute of Technology Jammu

**Reference No.:** IITJMU/Acad/2025/487  
**Date:** 12 June 2025

---

# Notification

## Subject: Minor Programme in Computer Science and Engineering

The **29th Senate** has approved the **Minor Programme in Computer Science and Engineering** under the **UG 2022 Curriculum**.

The minimum criteria for award of the Minor Degree shall be as per the institute norms.

---

# Minor Programme in Computer Science and Engineering

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Computer Science And Engineering

text:
## Overview

The programme provides students from non-CSE disciplines an opportunity to gain foundational and advanced knowledge in:

- Algorithms
- Computer Architecture
- Programming Languages
- Databases
- Operating Systems
- Computer Networks
- Artificial Intelligence
- Machine Learning
- Cyber Security
- Computer Vision
- Natural Language Processing
- High Performance Computing

---

# Minor in Engineering and Scientific Computation Dec 2020

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Engineering And Scientific Computation Dec 2020

text: <!-- Page 1 --> Proposal for Minor in Engineering and Scientific Computation A program offering minor in engineering and scientific computation is proposed for undergraduate students at IIT Jammu. The program is expected to provide the prospective students a platform to develop computational skills in modern numerical tools to model problems in various fields of engineering and engineering consulting. Undergraduate students completing the program will also be prepared to continue their masters or PhD programs in computational science and engineering, and relevant jobs at demand in the industry. Numerous streams of modern science and engineering benefit from computational techniques. Computational mechanics, for example, often combines experimental studies with theoretical modeling and numerical simulation to create tools that both explain phenomena and predict behavior and that may be used to advance concepts and designs in industry, involving but not limited to automobile, constructio

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Engineering And Scientific Computation Dec 2020

text: for preparing oneself for a specific/emerging direction. Conventional optional guiding paths offer the ease of starting with the select offered courses, where the prerequisites are automatically fulfilled by the core courses at IIT Jammu. At the same time, students have the freedom to take courses which they find interesting and relevant to enhance their computational skills. The courses in this program will be offered from 4th semester of students pursuing undergraduate degree. The program is aimed at developing skills in the students related to formulating and solving their engineering/science problems through computational means. Page 1 of 29

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Engineering And Scientific Computation Dec 2020

<!-- Page 2 -->
Program Structure:
An undergraduate student will earn a minor degree in engineering and scientific computation,
along with their major engineering degree provided the student satisfies the following criteria:
a) An aspiring student will need minimum 12 credits from the basket of courses to obtain
the minor degree in the said program.
b) Aspiring students can opt for the minor program from fourth semester onward and s/he
can choose at the most two courses in a semester.
c) The program offers one essential core course to inculcate the foundation, and the rest
are elective courses which can be selected by the students depending on their choice and
interest. These elective courses, for the advantage of the students, are grouped into
virtual guided threads with an aim to shape the student towards a specific direction of
application, employability, and/or research. These threads, though suggestive in nature,
do not restrict the students from taking path of their choice t

The main objectives of this course are:
• To identify the practical physical problem and able to model them mathematically.
• To solve the model problem using finite element method numerically.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
• Able to solve continuum boundary value problems and initial value problems encountered
in engineering by numerically.

Eigen value problems, Semi-discrete equation of motion, explicit and implicit algorithm.

P.T.O.
11. Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
The linear and nonlinear ordinary and partial differential governing equations associated with fluid
dynamics and heat transfer will be discretized and numerically solved in a time domain using finite
difference and finite volume methods. Discretization techniques will to be introduced through lecture
classes and those techniques need to be implemented through programming languages during practical
laboratory sessions. In-house computer programs are to be developed to solve and analyze real life
problems. Finally, the need for consistency, stability and convergence of the solution techniques will be
studied.
12. Course outcomes (about 100 words):
Fundamentals of finite difference and finite volume methods will be introduced. The students will be able
to discretize the equations for 1D and 2-D regular geometry using explicit and implicit techniques, and
develop in-house computer programs

hybrid, exponential and power law schemes.
6. Navier Stokes equations: Vorticity

# Minor in Entrepreneurship and Start-up

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Entrepreneurship And Start-Up

text:
<!-- Page 1 -->
Proposal for Minor Program in Entrepreneurship and Start-up
Introduction:
Entrepreneurship and startup culture have become integral components of today's dynamic economy,
fostering innovation, creativity, and job creation. To equip our students with the skills and mindset
necessary to thrive in this competitive landscape, we propose the establishment of a minor program in
Entrepreneurship and Startup within the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences.
Program Structure:
The minor program will offer students the flexibility to choose between two routes to earn the minor
degree: the academic route and the workshop route.
1. Academic Route:
o Students opting for the academic route will be required to complete four courses, starting
from the 5th semester.
o Two compulsory courses, ES1 (Introduction to Entrepreneurship) and ES2 (Startup
Fundamentals), must be completed.
o The remaining two courses, totaling 6 credits, will be selected from a basket of elective
courses o

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Entrepreneurship And Start-Up

text:
<!-- Page 2 -->
Conclusion:
The proposed minor program in Entrepreneurship and Startup aims to equip our students with the
knowledge, skills, and entrepreneurial mindset necessary to succeed in today's competitive business
landscape. By offering both academic and practical pathways, we can cater to the diverse interests and
learning styles of our students, preparing them to become innovative leaders and contributors to the
global economy.
List of courses for the Academic route:
Compulsory courses:
Entrepreneurship and Start-up- Part 1
Entrepreneurship and Start-up - Part 2
Basket of Elective Courses.
Corporate Law
Finance
Marketing
Industrial and Organisational Behaviour

# Minor in Mathematics

<!-- Page 1 -->
Proposal of Minor in Mathematics for UG students
The Department of Mathematics, IIT Jammu plans to offer a program ‘Minor in Mathematics’
for UG students in which they are expected to build strong mathematical foundations. It is well-
known that the mathematical methods are instrumental today in numerous career fields
including (but not limited to) medicine, law, design, engineering, finance, accounting,
economics, architecture, and computer programming. Mathematics is the backbone of several
engineering concepts. However, it is quite difficult to provide the detailed underlying
mathematical concepts during the courses (

| including (but not limited to) medicine, law, design, engineering, finance, accounting, |
| economics, architecture, and computer programming. Mathematics is the backbone of several |

others. This program, in particular, will be quite beneficial to those who wish to go for

2. Curriculum structure
The following are the broad guidelines subject to the approval of the competent authority.
● This minor program has been specifically designed for 2017, 2018 batches.
● A student needs to earn minimum 20 credits from the basket of courses to obtain the
minor degree in Mathematics.
● A student can opt for the minor program in Mathematics from fifth semester onwards
and he/she can choose at most 2 courses in a semester.
● The courses accounting for the minor degree need to be different from the courses
taken to complete the regular B.Tech. degree requirements.
● A student, with SGPA ≥ 7.00 in all the semesters starting from 2nd year onwards and
no backlog course till date, can start pursuing for a B.Tech. degree with minor.
● The student needs to maintain SGPA ≥ 7.00 in all the subsequent semesters

# Introduction

Quantum Technology is an emerging field that leverages the principles of quantum mechanics to develop transformative technologies.

Applications span a wide range of domains including:

- Quantum Computing
- Quantum Cryptography
- Quantum Communication
- Quantum Sensing
- Advanced Materials
- Precision Metrology

The Department of Physics at IIT Jammu proposes a **Minor Programme in Quantum

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Minor In Quantum Technology

text:

- Secure communication networks
- Quantum internet technologies

---

## Research and Innovation

Students may contribute to:

- Quantum information science
- Quantum hardware development
- Quantum algorithms
- Quantum materials
- Quantum sensing systems

The programme also encourages innovation in emerging quantum applications.

---

# Programme Objectives

The Minor Programme in Quantum Technology aims to:

## Objective I

Provide a foundational understanding of quantum mechanics and its technological applications.

Key topics include:

- Quantum superposition
- Quantum entanglement
- Quantum measurement

---

## Objective II

Introduce students to:

- Quantum computing
- Quantum algorithms
- Quantum information processing

Students will understand how quantum systems can solve problems beyond the capabilities of classical computers.

---

## Objective III

Develop understanding of:

- Quantum communication
- Quantum cryptography
- Secure communication prot

11. Course objectives(Why should one study this course?)(about 100 words):
The primary objective of this course is to understand the basic concepts of statistics and how
statistics helps in analyzing biological data by using simple examples. To prepare students to
handle biological data and to draw appropriate conclusions from the analyses.
12. Course outcomes(about 100 words):
Student will develop a clear understanding of measures of central tendency and measures of
dispersion, Coefficient of variation, skewness and kurtosis, coefficient of correlation. Learning
different probability discrete and continuous distributions and their implementation at realistic
models. Design of experiments, Basic concepts of hypothesis testing, including framing of null
and alternative hypothesis. Hypothesis testing

The major objective of this course is to acquaint students with the various aspects of industrial
microbiology and bioprocess engineering, different types of fermentation processes,
fermenters design and operations Students will become familiar with mass scale culturing of
microorganisms for industrial production of various bio-molecules, metabolites of industrial
interest and different recovery methods in detail.

bioprocess engineering. Students would learn about different types of fermentation processes
in liquid media as well as solid state substrates media. Students will learn about the design,
operation and uses of different types of fermenters of laboratory, pilot and industrial scale. Gain
insight into the techniques of isolation, screening, preservation and mainten

Proposal for Minor program in Bioengineering
The Centre of Biosciences and Bioengineering plans to offer a minor program in
Bioengineering to the UG students. The program offers forward-looking courses dedicated to
producing quality engineers who can match the growing needs and demands in the wide range
of bioengineering professions. This will be accomplished by developing and offering curricula
that can truly integrate engineering with biology and medical sciences. Combined with a strong
emphasis on research and hands-on training, the program is designed in a way that will
encourage students to address the open-ended problems of society.

solutions to them. To cater this, the program aims to create a workforce that will be skilled in

industrial microbiology, drug design, drug delivery, etc. Therefore, the employment
opportunities lie with (but is not limited to) pharma, food and beverages, and biomedical
industries. They should also be able to undertake medical research for understanding human
physiology, treating pathophysiology, etc.

11. (Course objectives (Why should one study this course?) (about 100 words):
This course will introduce the fundamental concepts of nanoscience in biology and medicine.
The course aims to enable students to understand how nanoscience can be useful in various bio
applications. The inter-disciplinary nature of this course allows students from varied background
to have a glimpse on the fascinating nano-bioscience. The course will cover basics of|
nanobiotechnology and will help student to develop an understanding of the application in
bioengineering thereof.

12. |Course outcomes (about 100 words):
After successful completion of the course, the students are expected to understand:
1. Fundamentals of Nanobiotechnology
2. Applications of Nanomaterials in Bioengineering
3. Develop understanding of the scope of innovation in Nanobiotechnology

# Specialization in VLSI

# Microelectronics and VLSI Design
## Minor / Specialization Programme

> **Document Type:** Minutes of Meeting (MoM)  
> **Department:** Electrical Engineering  
> **Annexure:** 3  
> **Meeting Date:** 16 April 2024

---

# Background

A meeting of subject experts from the Department of Electrical Engineering was held on **16 April 2024** to discuss the course structure for a proposed:

- Minor in Microelectronics and VLSI Design
- Specialization in Microelectronics and VLSI Design

The committee reviewed and finalized the list of courses to be included in the programme.

---

# Approved Course Basket

The following courses were

metadata.title: Curriculum Ug Specialization Specialization In Vlsi

text:
### Focus Areas

- Processor Architecture
- Hardware–Software Co-design
- Embedded Platforms
- System Integration

---

# Committee Recommendations

The committee discussed the following academic guidelines.

The detailed policy details are as mentioned below: Vision: B.Tech (Honors) by research is a program proposed to align with the envisaged vision of Viksit Bharat 2047 and National Education Policy, 2020 (NEP). An aspirational developing nation requires high-quality researchers that would engage in R&D for the nation’s empowerment and economic and technological independence. Aim: The aim of the proposed program is manifold, as enumerated below:  This program aims to provide students an opportunity to engage in diverse and inter-disciplinary fields of research that may span across the boundaries of departments and lead to a comprehensive technological research environment involving all branches of engineering and science in the institute.  The program encompasses the idea th

text: these semesters are tentative and will only appear in final transcripts upon completing and qualifying the proposed degree program at the end of 4 semesters. If a student decides to withdraw from the proposed program in between then the grades earned by the students will not be shown on the marksheet at the end of the program. The courses will be offered in the co-curricular category. These additional courses cannot be counted as open electives in case the student withdraws from B.Tech (Honors) or does not meet the awarding criteria. The course category for such courses will be Dissertation and will not be considered under the graduating requirements as part of OE/BTP. 4. It is optional for both the faculty members and students to participate. However, if a faculty member has agreed to participate in the degree program, they must ensure their availability to students over the span of 4 semesters. The faculty must ensure their availability during the span of the program, as minimum cont

metadata.title: Bs In Honors

text: <!-- Page 2 --> However, during the summer semester, the supervising faculty member should ensure at least 4-weeks of availability on campus. The accommodation and food charges for the student during that period will be borne by the faculty member through external project contingency or PDF or by the student. Program Management: 1. Associate Dean of the undergraduate programs (ADUG) will be the program coordinator and the DUGC of all departments will be the de-facto members of the committee. The committee members will assist the ADUG with the program's implementation. 2. At the beginning of every even semester semester, ADUG will float a form to all the faculty members seeking the title and at least 500 words description of the project they want to offer for the Honors degree. The description should contain the aim, objectives, possible approaches, and outcomes. The description should be detailed enough for the students to gauge the trade-offs of the extra effort they will put in over

text: to work with them on their project as ONLY one student can work in one project. The faculty member can ONLY select one student per project. The program committee will share the list of projects along with the student list within the next 3-weeks. For each student, it is mandatory that the supervisor or at least one co-supervisor is from the home department. 6. A faculty member cannot supervisor more than 3 UG students under this program (i.e., cannot offer more than 3 projects) 7. Once the allocation is complete, the faculty member will form the SRC committee for the student within next two weeks. The SRC request to go to DUGC of the student’s department and then to ADUG for final approval. 8. The total timeline spans 14-16 weeks, starting from the 1st week of January for the allocation of students to the program to be complete. The ADUG can engage the DUGC members to assist him/her in management of the program. 9. Not more than one course per semester can be registered for UG Honors p

text: <!-- Page 3 --> for the Honors to be awarded. 2. A student should have CGPA above 9, at the end of the 4th semester to opt and to receive UG (honors) and minor degree simultaneously. Student Incentivization: 1. Upon completion of the Honors degree, the degree will be conferred mentioning “B.Tech in XYZ Engineering with UG (Honors) by research” or “B.Tech in XYZ Engineering with UG (Honors) by Interdisciplinary research” (when students of one branch pursue research on the topic from the area related to another Department (the supervisor can certify the same)) . In case a student requires a separate document the same may be issued by the concerned faculty supervisor and HoD (parent department of student) like a recommendation letter. 2. # NOTIFICATION

## Subject: Conversion of "II" Grade

The Competent Authority has approved the following guidelines for the conversion of **"II" grade**.

---

# Guidelines for Conversion of "II" Grade

## 1. Award of "II" Grade

The **"II" grade** shall be awarded only in exceptional circumstances where a student is unable to appear for an examination due to:

- Medical reasons
- Emergency situations
- Other valid exceptional circumstances

### Documentation Requirement

Course coordinators must obtain documentary evidence from the student supporting the reason for absence.

metadata.title: Conversion Of Ii Grade

text:
### Result

> Any remaining **"II" grade** shall be automatically converted to **"FF" grade** by the EG System on the 16th day.

---

# Grade Conversion Workflow

```text
Student receives II Grade
            │
            ▼
Supporting documents submitted
            │
            ▼
Course Coordinator Review
            │
            ▼
Grading Window Reopens
      (Day 14–15)
            │
     ┌──────┴──────┐
     │             │
     ▼             ▼
Converted      Not Converted
to Point Grade      │
                    ▼
             Day 16 Automatic
             Conversion to FF
```

---

# Responsibilities

## Student

- Provide valid justification for absence.
- Submit supporting documentation promptly.
- Coordinate with the course instructor for completion of pending requirements.

## Course Coordinator

- Verify supporting documentation.
- Ensure timely evaluation.
- Convert the "II" grade within the prescribed timeline.

## Institute Doctor

- Issue or verify medical certificat

# Earn While yoU LEaRn scheme
# IIT Jammu Notification

**Reference:** IITJMU/Acad/2023/...  
**Date:** 16 January 2024

# EULER: Earn While yoU LEaRn (EULER) Scheme

## Introduction

The Competent Authority has approved the policy for the **EULER: Earn While yoU LEaRn Scheme** for the students of IIT Jammu.

The scheme aims to provide students with an opportunity to earn subsistence support through voluntary work on tasks identified by IIT Jammu. The scheme is intended to:

- Instill a sense of ownership and belongingness among students.
- Acknowledge the dignity of labour.
- Boost self-confidence.
- Provide hands-on experience with existing workflows and services.
- Prepare students for future jobs.
- Encourage

metadata.title: Earn While You Learn Scheme

text: ### Administrative Responsibilities The host department/section shall: - Maintain engagement records. - Forward verified monthly claims for payment. - Report misconduct or grievances immediately to the Academic Affairs Section. - Ensure that all engaged candidates follow institutional values and ethics. --- # Instructions for Candidates All UG, PG, and PhD students are eligible subject to the following conditions: ## Eligibility Conditions A student must: - Be enrolled in a full-time regular academic programme of IIT Jammu. - Have a CGPA of **8.0 or above**. - Not have received the degree for which they are enrolled. - Not be receiving any scholarship, fellowship, stipend, remuneration, or financial support from inside or outside the Institute. ## Conduct Requirements - Candidates must not have been subjected to disciplinary action. - If disciplinary action is discovered during the engagement period, benefits will be terminated immediately and the student will become ineligible for fut

metadata.title: Earn While You Learn Scheme

text: work funded through project grants (UG/PG/PhD) | ₹250 per hour | ₹10,000 (40 hours) | --- # EULER Engagement Flow ## Activity Identification Each Host Department, Section, or Faculty shall identify activities suitable under the scheme. These activities may be: - Department-specific. - Section-specific. - General institutional activities. However: - Departments must justify the necessity of such activities. - Normal duties of staff members cannot be offloaded to EULER candidates. - Regular PG/PhD scholars receiving MoE scholarships must be utilized first for TA, academic, and laboratory responsibilities. Non-academic activities may be assigned independently of MoE scholarship holders. --- ## Proposal Submission All proposals shall be: - Deliberated within the concerned department or section. - Properly documented through minutes. - Submitted by: - HoD, - Section In-Charge, or - Faculty concerned. The minutes must explicitly justify the requirement for EULER candidates. --- ## Selection

metadata.title: Earn While You Learn Scheme

text:
### Stage 1: Call for Activities

The Academic Section will invite activity proposals from host departments and sections.

### Stage 2: Approval

A Standing Committee will scrutinize and approve activities on a semester basis.

### Stage 3: Application Call

The Academic Section will invite applications from students.

### Stage 4: Departmental Screening

Applications will be forwarded to host departments.

Departments will:

- Scrutinize applications.
- Shortlist candidates.
- Recommend candidates to the Standing Committee.

### Stage 5: Interview (if required)

The selection process may include an interview conducted by a committee comprising at least three members, including:

- Supervisor / Concerned Assistant Registrar
- DPGC / DUGC / Concerned Associate Dean representative
- One additional faculty member (Associate Professor or above) or officer (Deputy Registrar or above)

At least one member must be at the level of **Associate Professor or above**.

### Stage 6: Final Approval
